proposals.title: Inverse Catalyst Design for Challenging Hydrogenations via Metal-Ligand and Metal-Metal Cooperativity
proposals.motivation: The rational design of catalysts for difficult transformations, such as the direct hydrogenation of amides or nitriles to amines under mild conditions, remains a grand challenge. While Milstein's work on metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) and template catalysis has shown immense promise, the discovery of new cooperative motifs, particularly in multimetallic systems, is largely serendipitous. A computational framework to reverse-engineer catalyst structures from a desired reaction profile would dramatically accelerate this discovery process.
proposals.hypothesis: That a computational inverse-design workflow, trained on known MLC and template catalysis principles, can predict novel multimetallic pincer complexes with tailored cooperative sites capable of catalyzing a specific, difficult hydrogenation reaction not efficiently catalyzed by known systems.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative model using a variational autoencoder (VAE) architecture to produce candidate 3D catalyst structures. The model will be conditioned on target reaction descriptors (e.g., activation energy, intermediate stability) derived from DFT calculations for a chosen difficult transformation (e.g., N2O hydrogenation or base-free nitrile coupling). A reinforcement learning loop, guided by DFT-calculated energy profiles, will iteratively refine the VAE's latent space to favor structures exhibiting strong metal-(thiol) ligand and metal-metal cooperativity, as inspired by Milstein's recent mechanistic studies.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational methodology for catalyst discovery and a shortlist of predicted, synthetically accessible multimetallic complexes for experimental validation. It will provide fundamental insights into the design rules for effective cooperativity in hydrogenation catalysis, moving the field from serendipitous discovery towards true rational design.
proposals.novelty: This approach inverts the traditional computational screening paradigm by generating catalysts to fit a predefined reaction pathway, explicitly focusing on the cooperative effects central to Milstein's work but extending them into the underexplored domain of designed multimetallic systems.

proposals.title: Ambient-Temperature Nitrile Coupling via Metal–Ligand Cooperation with Transient Thiolate Ligands
proposals.motivation: The Milstein group's recent discovery of manganese-catalyzed nitrile coupling via template catalysis represents a significant advance in sustainable base-metal catalysis. However, the requirement for elevated temperatures (e.g., 80-120°C) limits functional group tolerance and energy efficiency. Achieving such C–C bond formations under ambient conditions remains a major, unsolved challenge in catalysis, with profound implications for synthesizing fine chemicals and pharmaceuticals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the principles of metal–ligand cooperation (MLC) with the use of thiols as transient cooperative ligands will dramatically lower the kinetic barrier for nitrile activation and coupling, enabling the reaction to proceed efficiently at room temperature.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a new library of pincer Mn(I) and Fe(II) complexes featuring flanking thioether or selenoether donors, known to facilitate MLC. These will be screened in the coupling of saturated and unsaturated nitriles at 25°C, with catalytic amounts of aromatic thiols (e.g., PhSH) as transient ligands. Reaction progress will be monitored by in situ IR and NMR spectroscopy to identify key intermediates. The mechanism will be probed via deuterium labeling studies, kinetic analysis, and isolation of proposed metal–hydride and metal–thiolate species.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first general, ambient-temperature catalytic system for the direct coupling of nitriles, significantly expanding the synthetic utility of this transformation. It will provide fundamental insights into how transient ligand participation can be harnessed to accelerate challenging bond activations under exceptionally mild conditions.
proposals.novelty: While MLC and thiol acceleration effects are known individually, their synergistic combination to achieve nitrile coupling at room temperature is unprecedented. This moves beyond simple ligand design to a dynamic, co-catalytic strategy for radical rate enhancement.

proposals.title: Catalytic Tandem Dehydrogenation-Hydrogenation via Metal-Ligand Cooperative H2 Liberation and Capture
proposals.motivation: Metal-ligand cooperative systems, particularly those involving thiols or other heteroatom donors, are known to facilitate reversible H2 activation and transfer, enabling (de)hydrogenation catalysis. However, stoichiometric H2 liberation in such systems is often a terminal step, wasting a valuable reducing equivalent. Efficiently coupling this liberated H2 to a concurrent, productive hydrogenation step would create atom-economical tandem catalytic processes, minimizing waste and energy input in organic synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a designed pincer complex, utilizing metal-ligand cooperation (e.g., via an aromatization/dearomatization or thiol/thiolate couple), can stoichiometrically liberate H2 from a substrate while simultaneously activating the same H2 molecule for the hydrogenation of a distinct, unsaturated substrate present in the same pot.
proposals.method: We will synthesize manganese and calcium pincer complexes bearing cooperative ligands (e.g., pyridine-based PNP or NNN with pendant thiol/amine groups) known for H2 activation. Using pre-2026 established protocols, we will first study the stoichiometric dehydrogenation of model substrates like alcohols or amines, confirming H2 liberation via gas evolution and NMR. Concurrently, we will introduce hydrogenation acceptors (e.g., imines, alkenes, quinoline) to the reaction mixture. Reaction progress will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR spectroscopy. Key experiments will involve isotopic labeling (D2, deuterated substrates) and kinetic studies to trace hydrogen transfer and establish the catalytic cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a proof-of-concept for a fully integrated, acceptorless dehydrogenation coupled with hydrogenation using a single catalyst, where the liberated H2 is the direct reductant. It will expand the synthetic utility of metal-ligand cooperation beyond simple transfer hydrogenation, contributing to the development of more efficient, redox-neutral catalytic cascades. The findings will provide fundamental insights into the kinetics and mechanisms of simultaneous H2 liberation and capture.
proposals.novelty: While Milstein's group has shown thiols can accelerate or inhibit (de)hydrogenation and that calcium complexes can activate H2, the direct, in-situ coupling of stoichiometric H2 production from one substrate to the hydrogenation of another in a closed system using the same catalyst is a novel, non-obvious application of these principles, moving from sequential or transfer processes to a true tandem catalytic manifold.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis for Direct Amidation of Alcohols Using Alternative Hydrogen Donors
proposals.motivation: The catalytic amidation of alcohols using borrowing hydrogen or hydrogen autotransfer catalysis is a pivotal atom-economical route to amides. However, these reactions inherently require the alcohol substrate to serve as both the acyl source and the hydrogen donor, limiting substrate scope and functional group tolerance. Developing systems where the alcohol is solely the acyl source, with hydrogen shuttled from an external, benign donor, would unlock new synthetic pathways and enable the use of alcohols bearing sensitive functionalities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pincer-ligated metal complexes, operating via metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) or employing thiols as transient cooperative ligands, can efficiently catalyze the dehydrogenation of an alcohol to an aldehyde intermediate while utilizing a distinct, easily oxidized hydrogen donor (e.g., formic acid, glycerol, or H2) to drive the subsequent reductive amination step.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and screen a library of Mn, Fe, and Ru pincer complexes known for MLC, particularly those with thiol/thiolate or amine/amide cooperative sites. Catalytic testing will involve reacting benzyl alcohol derivatives with primary amines in the presence of a stoichiometric alternative hydrogen donor (e.g., formic acid) under base-free conditions. Reaction progress will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR. Mechanistic studies, including deuterium labeling, in-situ NMR, and isolation of potential intermediates, will be conducted to delineate the roles of the alcohol and the external donor in the catalytic cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new catalytic paradigm for amide synthesis, decoupling the acyl source from the hydrogen donor role. It will expand the toolbox of sustainable catalysis by Milstein-type systems and provide fundamental insights into the interplay between different hydrogen donors and metal-ligand cooperative platforms. The methodology could lead to more selective and efficient transformations for pharmaceutical and polymer chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While MLC is well-established for (de)hydrogenation, its application to explicitly partition the roles of alcohol (acyl source) and a separate hydrogen donor in amidation is unexplored. The use of thiols as transient ligands to modulate hydrogen transfer between two distinct substrates represents a novel extension of recent pre-2026 findings from the Milstein group.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Cleavage of Amides to Alcohols and Amines via Catalytic Dehydrative Hydrogenation
proposals.motivation: The amide bond is exceptionally stable, making its formation from amines and alcohols under mild conditions a major challenge. Conversely, the reverse reaction—cleaving amides to regenerate the alcohol and amine—is a fundamental but difficult transformation with significant potential for chemical recycling and the deconstruction of polyamides. Pre-2026, catalytic strategies for this bond scission are rare and typically require harsh conditions or stoichiometric reagents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bifunctional pincer complexes, leveraging metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) with thiol or other protic functional groups, can catalyze the dehydrative hydrogenation of amides, cleaving the C–N bond with H2 to release the constituent alcohol and amine.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Mn, Fe, and Ru pincer complexes featuring thiol, amine, or hydroxy groups on the ligand backbone for MLC. These catalysts will be screened in the reaction of model amides with H2 (1-50 bar) at elevated temperatures (100-180°C). Key parameters include solvent polarity, base additives, and catalyst loading. Reaction progress will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR, with mechanistic studies (kinetics, deuterium labeling, isolation of intermediates) to elucidate the role of the cooperative ligand in facilitating amide activation and C–N bond cleavage.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind catalytic system for the direct hydrogenative cleavage of amides, expanding the repertoire of MLC beyond (de)hydrogenation of N-heterocycles and imines. It will provide fundamental insights into the activation of robust bonds via cooperative catalysis and open a new route for depolymerization and chemical synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While MLC pincer complexes are known for (de)hydrogenation, their application to reverse amide formation—a thermodynamically uphill process under mild conditions—via dehydrative hydrogenation is unprecedented. The use of thiols as transient cooperative ligands to potentially accelerate this challenging bond cleavage is a novel mechanistic direction.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Dehydrogenation of Alcohols via Metal–Ligand Cooperative Relay: Merging Molecular Pincer Complexes with Heterogeneous Electron Transfer
proposals.motivation: Milstein's pre-2026 work established metal–ligand cooperation (MLC) as a powerful paradigm for (de)hydrogenation catalysis, including the use of thiols as transient cooperative ligands. However, these homogeneous systems rely on precious metal catalysts and often require stoichiometric chemical oxidants or hydrogen acceptors. Developing more sustainable and atom-economical methods for alcohol oxidation, a critical transformation in organic synthesis, remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the dehydrogenation step in MLC cycles, typically mediated by a metal center, can be replaced by direct electrochemical oxidation at an anode, with a redox-active pincer complex serving as an electron–proton relay to shuttle hydrides from the substrate to the electrode surface.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of known and novel pincer complexes of earth-abundant metals (Mn, Fe, Ca) featuring MLC-active sites (e.g., dearomatized pyridine, thiol/thiolate, amido/amine). These will be evaluated as molecular mediators in an electrochemical cell under base-free conditions. The anode material (e.g., carbon, Pt) and potential will be optimized for the oxidation of benzylic and aliphatic alcohols to carbonyl compounds. In-situ spectroscopic techniques (FT-IR, UV-Vis) and controlled-potential electrolysis will be used to probe the formation of key metal-hydride intermediates and establish the relay mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new conceptual framework for electrocatalysis by integrating well-defined molecular MLC principles with heterogeneous electron transfer. It will demonstrate a transition-metal-catalyst-free method for selective alcohol oxidation, expanding the toolbox of sustainable electrocatalytic reactions. The findings will provide fundamental insights into the interplay between molecular coordination chemistry and electrode surfaces.
proposals.novelty: While electrochemical alcohol oxidation is known, the use of a designed pincer complex not as a catalyst but as a stoichiometric or regenerative electron–proton relay mediator, explicitly exploiting its MLC architecture for substrate activation distinct from the electrode event, is a novel and non-obvious strategy derived from Milstein's molecular insights.

proposals.title: Scalable, Base-Free Amide Synthesis via Manganese Pincer Catalysis: Process Development and Mechanistic Insights for Industrial Application
proposals.motivation: Amides are fundamental motifs in pharmaceuticals, polymers, and agrochemicals, yet their synthesis often relies on stoichiometric, waste-generating reagents or precious metal catalysts. The Milstein group's recent discovery of a manganese-catalyzed, base-free addition of nitriles (template catalysis) offers a sustainable, atom-economical route to amides using an earth-abundant metal. Transitioning this elegant laboratory reaction to a viable industrial process remains a critical, unsolved challenge that bridges fundamental organometallic chemistry with practical chemical engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the manganese pincer template catalysis system can be engineered for continuous, large-scale operation by optimizing catalyst stability, substrate scope for industrially relevant feedstocks, and reactor design, while the underlying metal-ligand and thiol cooperative mechanisms provide levers to inhibit deactivation pathways under process conditions.
proposals.method: We will first conduct batch reactor studies to identify catalyst decomposition pathways and optimize the ligand (Pincer) and thiol additive structure for longevity and activity with technical-grade substrates. Building on Milstein's mechanistic work on thiol acceleration/inhibition, we will use in-situ spectroscopy (IR, NMR) to monitor the catalytic cycle under process-relevant concentrations and temperatures. Subsequently, we will design and test a continuous flow reactor system to improve heat and mass transfer, facilitate catalyst recycling via immobilization strategies, and enable the safe handling of reactive nitrile intermediates at scale.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a blueprint for the scalable production of amides via manganese catalysis, moving beyond proof-of-concept to address practical engineering constraints. It will provide fundamental insights into the stability and deactivation mechanisms of cooperative pincer systems under industrially relevant conditions, a topic rarely explored in molecular catalysis research.
proposals.novelty: While the catalytic reaction is known, the systematic process development for industrial scale, integrating mechanistic understanding of metal-ligand-thiol cooperation to solve stability issues, is unprecedented. This merges the precision of molecular catalysis with the pragmatic demands of process chemistry.

proposals.title: Manganese-Catalyzed Dehydrogenative Coupling of Alcohols to Esters via Metal-Ligand and Calcium-Ligand Cooperation
proposals.motivation: The synthesis of esters from alcohols via dehydrogenative coupling offers a direct, atom-economical, and oxidant-free route, avoiding the use of carboxylic acid derivatives and generating only H2 as a byproduct. Despite its appeal, this transformation remains challenging, often requiring high catalyst loadings, strong bases, or precious metals. Building on the Milstein group's pre-2026 expertise in base-free catalysis and metal-ligand cooperation, this project seeks to develop a more sustainable and efficient catalytic system.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that earth-abundant manganese or calcium complexes, featuring cooperative pincer or thiol-derived ligands, can catalyze the direct coupling of two alcohol molecules to form esters and H2 by facilitating sequential alcohol dehydrogenation and aldehyde–alcohol cross-coupling steps.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and screen a library of Mn(I) and Ca(II) complexes bearing pyridine- and acridine-based PNP/NNN pincer ligands, as well as ligands with pendant thiol groups, inspired by recent work on thiols as transient cooperative ligands. Catalytic testing will involve heating primary alcohols (e.g., benzyl alcohol, 1-hexanol) under base-free, inert atmosphere conditions, monitoring ester formation and H2 evolution via GC and GC-MS. Mechanistic studies will include stoichiometric reactions, isolation of potential intermediates (e.g., metal-hydrides, hemiaminals), and deuterium labeling experiments to probe the hydrogen transfer pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, base-free catalytic method for ester synthesis using earth-abundant metals, contributing to the principles of sustainable catalysis. It will expand the scope of metal-ligand cooperation, particularly for calcium and with thiol auxiliaries, and provide detailed mechanistic insights into dehydrogenative coupling cycles.
proposals.novelty: While the Milstein group has demonstrated dehydrogenative coupling for amide and imine synthesis, its application to ester formation with Mn or Ca catalysts is unexplored. The proposed use of thiol cooperative ligands to potentially accelerate key steps, such as hemiaminal dehydration, represents a novel strategic element borrowed from recent inhibition/acceleration studies.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis for Carbonate Hydrogenation Using Low-Pressure H2 or Alternative Hydrogen Donors
proposals.motivation: The catalytic hydrogenation of carbonates to methanol and diols is a promising route for CO2 utilization and renewable chemical synthesis, but state-of-the-art systems typically require high pressures of molecular hydrogen (H2), posing significant energy and safety costs. Developing catalysts that operate under mild conditions with low H2 pressure or alternative hydrogen sources would greatly enhance the practicality and sustainability of this transformation. This aligns with the global pre-2026 push towards energy-efficient and atom-economical catalytic processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pincer-ligand frameworks, particularly those featuring metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) with thiol or amine groups, can activate and transfer hydrogen from low-pressure H2 or organic donors to carbonates via low-energy pathways, circumventing the need for high H2 pressures.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Mn, Fe, and Ca complexes based on Milstein's established PNP and PNS pincer platforms, where the ligand backbone incorporates cooperative -SH or -NH functionalities. These complexes will be screened as catalysts for dimethyl carbonate hydrogenation under systematically varied H2 pressures (1-20 bar) and temperatures (80-150°C). Parallel screening will employ alternative hydrogen donors (e.g., iPrOH, formic acid, ammonia-borane) under H2-free conditions. Reaction kinetics and inhibition studies, informed by Milstein's recent work on thiol acceleration/inhibition effects, will be used to elucidate the operative mechanism and identify the most effective MLC system.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first catalytic systems for carbonate hydrogenation under industrially more viable, low-pressure H2 conditions. It will provide fundamental insights into how MLC with transient donors modulates reactivity in hydrogenation of challenging C-O bonds, expanding the scope of MLC beyond N-heterocycle and alkene hydrogenation. The findings could establish a new paradigm for hydrogenation catalysis using earth-abundant metals under mild conditions.
proposals.novelty: While MLC pincer complexes are known for (de)hydrogenation, their application to carbonate hydrogenation is unexplored. The strategic use of thiol/amine cooperative ligands to replace high-pressure H2 requirements with mechanistic tricks derived from inhibition studies is a novel and non-obvious approach.

proposals.title: Enhancing Stability and Activity in Manganese Pincer Catalysis: Ligand Backbone and Cooperative Thiol Modifications for Nitrile Addition
proposals.motivation: The Milstein group's recent discovery of a manganese-catalyzed, base-free addition of saturated to unsaturated nitriles via template catalysis represents a significant advance in sustainable, earth-abundant metal catalysis. However, for practical synthetic and potential industrial application, catalyst longevity and turnover number (TON) under demanding process conditions are critical bottlenecks. Addressing these stability and activity limitations is essential to transition this promising methodology from a laboratory proof-of-concept to a robust synthetic tool.
proposals.hypothesis: Strategic modifications to the pincer ligand backbone, informed by principles of metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), coupled with the rational use of transient cooperative thiol additives, will significantly enhance the manganese catalyst's stability against decomposition pathways and increase its overall turnover number in nitrile addition reactions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of Mn(I) PNP and PNN pincer complexes featuring systematic variations in the ligand backbone (e.g., alkyl vs. aryl linkers, incorporation of heteroatoms, steric bulk). Their catalytic performance in the model nitrile addition will be benchmarked against the original catalyst under standardized conditions. Concurrently, we will screen a series of thiols (aryl, alkyl, functionalized) as transient cooperative ligands, building on the group's recent mechanistic insights into thiol acceleration/inhibition effects in (de)hydrogenation. Catalyst decomposition pathways will be identified via in-situ NMR and EPR spectroscopy, and the most stable/active systems will be subjected to extended reaction runs and substrate scope expansion to measure ultimate TONs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver new, more robust manganese pincer catalysts with demonstrably higher TONs for C–C bond forming reactions, directly addressing a key limitation in base-metal catalysis. It will provide fundamental structure-activity-stability relationships for Mn pincer complexes and expand the understanding of how ligand backbone engineering and transient cooperative ligands can be synergistically used to modulate catalyst lifetime.
proposals.novelty: While MLC is well-established in the Milstein group, its targeted application to solve stability issues in a templated C–C coupling reaction, combined with the deliberate exploitation of transient thiol effects—typically studied in (de)hydrogenation—for catalyst stabilization in this context, is a novel and non-obvious strategy.

proposals.title: Light-Driven Hydrogenation of Organic Carbonates via Metal-Ligand Cooperative Ruthenium Pincer Complexes
proposals.motivation: The catalytic reduction of organic carbonates to alcohols and methanol is a valuable but challenging transformation, typically requiring harsh conditions or strong reductants. Prior to 2026, Milstein's work has established metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) with pincer complexes as a powerful platform for (de)hydrogenation, yet its application to carbonate reduction remains underexplored. Merging this with photoredox catalysis, a rapidly growing pre-2026 field, offers a potential route to drive this demanding reaction under mild, base-free conditions using light as the primary energy input.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a ruthenium pincer complex featuring a thiol/thiolate cooperative ligand, when coupled with a suitable photoredox sensitizer under visible light irradiation, will generate a highly reactive metal-hydride intermediate capable of reducing the C–O bond in organic carbonates efficiently at room temperature.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize Ru(II) pincer complexes with thiol/thiolate arms, analogous to Milstein's pre-2026 systems for thiol-mediated acceleration. These will be paired with established organic photoredox catalysts (e.g., fac-Ir(ppy)3) or inorganic sensitizers. The reaction will be tested on model substrates like ethylene carbonate and dimethyl carbonate under blue LED irradiation, using H2 or iPrOH as the ultimate hydrogen source. Detailed mechanistic studies, including quenching experiments, luminescence spectroscopy, and identification of key Ru-H intermediates via NMR and EPR, will be conducted to elucidate the photo-initiated MLC cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, mild photocatalytic method for the reductive functionalization of carbonates, expanding the synthetic utility of Milstein's MLC paradigm. It will provide fundamental insights into how photoredox cycles can be integrated with cooperative ligand platforms to access reactive species for challenging bond activations, potentially inspiring applications beyond hydrogenation.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox catalysis and MLC are both established pre-2026, their synergistic merger for carbonate reduction is unprecedented. The novelty lies in using light to trigger the formation of a cooperative ligand-based metal-hydride from a dormant precursor, bypassing the need for strong bases or high temperatures typically required for such transformations.

proposals.title: Pilot-Scale Methanol Synthesis via a Calcium-Pincer Catalyst: Process Design and Stability Assessment under Industrially Relevant Conditions
proposals.motivation: Industrial methanol synthesis relies on Cu/ZnO/Al2O3 catalysts operating under high pressure (50-100 bar) and temperature (200-300°C), an energy-intensive process. Milstein's recent pre-2026 discovery of a calcium-pincer complex capable of hydrogenating ketones and imines under mild conditions suggests a potential paradigm shift. Demonstrating the scalability and robustness of this earth-abundant metal catalyst for CO/CO2 hydrogenation to methanol is a critical step toward sustainable chemical manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: The calcium-ligand cooperative system, which activates H2 and facilitates hydrogenation, can be engineered into a continuous-flow catalytic process for methanol synthesis that maintains activity and selectivity at pilot scale, offering a lower-pressure alternative to conventional technology.
proposals.method: We will design a continuous stirred-tank reactor (CSTR) pilot plant process based on kinetic data from Milstein's molecular catalyst. The core method involves immobilizing the calcium-pincer complex on a tailored silica support to prevent deactivation and enable continuous flow. Process parameters (pressure: 10-30 bar, temperature: 120-180°C, gas hourly space velocity) will be optimized for a syngas (CO/CO2/H2) feed. Long-term stability (>500 hours on-stream) and catalyst leaching will be rigorously monitored using ICP-MS and in-situ IR spectroscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first engineering-scale validation of a main-group metal molecular catalyst for methanol production, bridging homogeneous catalysis and chemical process engineering. It will yield critical data on catalyst lifetime, turnover numbers under flow, and a comparative techno-economic analysis against the commercial Cu/ZnO process.
proposals.novelty: While Milstein's calcium catalyst is novel for hydrogenation, its application in a continuous process for C1 feedstock conversion is unexplored. The integration of metal-ligand cooperation with reactor engineering for a base-metal methanol catalyst is a non-obvious and high-risk/high-reward approach.

proposals.title: Mechanistic Elucidation of Acid-Modulated Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis via Advanced Kinetic and Spectroscopic Analysis
proposals.motivation: The role of acidic additives in metal-ligand cooperative catalysis, particularly in (de)hydrogenation, is often reduced to a simple rate enhancement. However, pre-2026 work from Milstein's group and others shows acids can act as co-catalysts, proton shuttles, or resting state modifiers, with profound but poorly understood effects on the catalytic cycle. A systematic, mechanistic understanding is needed to move beyond empirical optimization and rationally design acid-modulated catalytic systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled acidic additives do not simply accelerate a single rate-limiting step but selectively modulate the thermodynamics and kinetics of multiple elementary steps within a metal-ligand cooperative cycle, fundamentally altering the catalytic mechanism and resting state.
proposals.method: We will employ Milstein's established Mn and Ca pincer complexes catalyzing nitrile addition or imine hydrogenation as model systems. Detailed kinetic analysis using variable time normalization analysis (VTNA), Eyring plots, and deuterium kinetic isotope effects will be performed with systematically varied Brønsted acid strength and concentration. In-situ NMR and IR spectroscopy, including parahydrogen-induced polarization (PHIP) for H2 activation steps, will identify intermediates and catalyst resting states under catalytic conditions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a comprehensive mechanistic map of acid modulation in cooperative catalysis, distinguishing between co-catalytic, shuttling, and inhibitory roles. It will deliver a predictive framework for using acids as precise levers in catalyst design, enabling the development of more efficient and selective base-free catalytic processes.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying advanced kinetic methodologies (VTNA) and in-situ spectroscopy not just to confirm rate enhancement, but to deconvolute and quantify acid's effect on each elementary step within a well-defined metal-ligand cooperative framework, moving beyond the binary 'acid-good' paradigm.

proposals.title: Acid-Modulated Metal–Ligand Cooperation: Using Controlled Protonation to Switch and Enhance Catalytic Pathways in Base-Free (De)Hydrogenation
proposals.motivation: The Milstein group's pioneering work on metal–ligand cooperation (MLC) has established base-free catalysis as a powerful paradigm, often relying on ligand dearomatization/aromatization or transient cooperative ligands like thiols. However, the role of exogenous Brønsted acids in these ostensibly base-free systems remains underexplored. Understanding how controlled protonation influences MLC pathways could reveal new modes of reactivity and address limitations in substrate scope or rate, a key challenge in pre-2026 catalytic hydrogenation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the strategic addition of non-coordinating Brønsted acids will not inhibit but rather modulate or accelerate base-free (de)hydrogenation catalysis by reversibly protonating key intermediates, thereby altering the energetics of the MLC cycle or enabling alternative, acid-assisted pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ Milstein's established pincer complexes (e.g., Ru, Mn, Ca-based) known for base-free hydrogenation of nitriles, imines, and alkenes. Catalytic reactions will be conducted under standard base-free conditions with systematic titration of acidic additives (e.g., [H(OEt2)2][BArF4], ammonium salts). Reaction progress will be monitored by NMR and GC, with kinetics analyzed to distinguish acceleration from inhibition. In-situ NMR and DFT studies (pre-2026 methods) will probe the protonation sites (metal vs. ligand vs. substrate) and the resultant changes in the catalytic mechanism.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish 'acid-modulated MLC' as a new conceptual framework, expanding the toolbox for controlling selectivity and activity in sustainable catalysis. It will provide mechanistic insights into how external protons interact with cooperative platforms, potentially leading to more robust and tunable catalysts for challenging bond activations.
proposals.novelty: While acids are typically avoided in base-free systems, this proposal deliberately introduces them as a strategic variable, challenging the conventional boundary between base-free and acid-assisted catalysis within a cooperative ligand framework. The focus on using acids to *enhance* MLC, rather than shut it down, is counter-intuitive given the field's emphasis on basic ligand sites.

proposals.title: Base-Free Catalytic Hydrogenation of Polar Bonds via Metal-Ligand Cooperation with Designed Pincer-Thiol Complexes
proposals.motivation: In homogeneous catalysis, the frequent requirement for stoichiometric strong bases (e.g., alkali metal carbonates) to activate substrates or generate active catalysts adds cost, complexity, and waste, limiting atom economy and complicating product purification. Recent work, including from the Milstein group, has demonstrated that metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) and the use of transient cooperative ligands like thiols can fundamentally alter catalytic pathways and efficiencies. This proposal seeks to unify these concepts to eliminate the external base requirement entirely.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rationally designed pincer-ligated transition metal complex, incorporating a thiol/thiolate moiety as an integral part of the ligand scaffold, will enable base-free catalytic hydrogenation of polar bonds (e.g., C=O, C=N) via a bifunctional activation mechanism where the metal and the thiol site cooperatively activate both H2 and the substrate.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of new PNN-type pincer ligands featuring a pendant thiol group in the ligand backbone, derived from amino-thiol precursors, and complex them with earth-abundant metals (Mn, Fe) relevant to Milstein's recent work. These complexes will be fully characterized (NMR, X-ray). Their catalytic activity will be rigorously tested in the hydrogenation of benchmark substrates (ketones, imines, nitriles) under strictly base-free conditions. Reaction progress, kinetics, and mechanistic details will be probed via in-situ spectroscopy (IR, NMR), deuterium labeling, and isolation of potential intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of robust, base-free catalysts for sustainable hydrogenation, expanding the toolbox of MLC. It will provide fundamental insights into the role of thiols as permanent, integrated cooperative ligands, distinguishing their mechanism from transient, additive systems. The findings will advance the design principles for next-generation catalysts that operate without stoichiometric additives.
proposals.novelty: While thiols have been shown as transient additives, their permanent, strategic incorporation into a pincer ligand framework to intrinsically obviate the need for any external base in hydrogenation catalysis is a novel and non-obvious design. This moves beyond additive effects to create a fundamentally new catalyst architecture.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Mechanism of Metal-Ligand Cooperation in Thiol-Functionalized Pincer Complexes: A Combined Kinetic and Spectroscopic Study
proposals.motivation: The Milstein group's recent discovery of thiols as transient cooperative ligands in (de)hydrogenation catalysis represents a significant advance in metal-ligand cooperation (MLC). However, the precise catalytic cycle, the nature of key intermediates, and the origin of the observed acceleration/inhibition effects remain poorly understood. A detailed mechanistic understanding is crucial for rationally designing next-generation catalysts with enhanced activity and selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the catalytic cycle involves a reversible, substrate-dependent thiol coordination/dissociation equilibrium, and that the observed rate modulation stems from the thiol's dual role as a proton shuttle and a hemilabile ligand influencing the metal's electronic state and coordination sphere.
proposals.method: We will perform detailed kinetic studies (initial rates, variable time normalization analysis, Eyring/Arrhenius plots) on a model hydrogenation reaction catalyzed by a well-defined Mn or Fe pincer complex with a thiol-appended arm. In situ and operando spectroscopic techniques, including low-temperature NMR, IR, and UV-Vis, will be employed to trap and characterize proposed intermediates. Isotopic labeling (D2, deuterated thiols) and kinetic isotope effects will be used to probe H2 activation and proton-transfer steps. Stoichiometric reactions of isolated catalyst species with substrates will validate proposed elementary steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive mechanistic map for thiol-mediated MLC, defining the interplay between ligand dynamics and catalytic function. It will establish design principles for using transient cooperative ligands, moving beyond static pincer frameworks. The findings will directly inform the development of more efficient base-free catalysts for sustainable chemical synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While MLC in pincer complexes is well-established, the mechanistic dissection of *transient* ligand cooperation, where an exogenous molecule (thiol) dynamically enters and exits the coordination sphere to enable catalysis, is a novel and unexplored frontier. This study moves from phenomenological observation to a fundamental kinetic and spectroscopic description of this dynamic process.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperation in Pincer Complexes for the Catalytic Dehydrogenative Coupling of Sterically Hindered Alcohols and Amines
proposals.motivation: The catalytic dehydrogenative coupling of alcohols and amines to form amides and imines is a vital atom-economical route, but current systems struggle with sterically demanding or electronically deactivated substrates, limiting their synthetic utility. Milstein's pre-2026 work on metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly with thiols and calcium, has demonstrated remarkable substrate activation capabilities. This proposal aims to directly address this limitation by leveraging these MLC principles to develop catalysts for challenging substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pincer complexes featuring tailored metal-ligand cooperative sites, particularly those involving secondary coordination sphere thiols or heterobimetallic motifs, will provide the necessary steric accommodation and electronic modulation to activate and couple hindered alcohols and amines under base-free conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Mn, Fe, and Ca pincer complexes with modified ligand frameworks, systematically varying steric bulk (e.g., t-butyl, adamantyl) and electronic properties (e.g., electron-donating/withdrawing groups) on the pincer arms and cooperative sites. These catalysts will be screened in the dehydrogenative coupling of model hindered substrates (e.g., 2,2,2-triphenylethanol, N-methylaniline) and electronically deactivated ones (e.g., p-nitrobenzyl alcohol). Reaction parameters (temperature, solvent, catalyst loading) will be optimized using high-throughput experimentation techniques, with a focus on achieving activity without exogenous base.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver new, efficient catalytic systems for transforming valuable but recalcitrant substrate classes, expanding the scope of sustainable amide and imine synthesis. It will provide fundamental insights into how steric and electronic parameters in MLC catalysts can be tuned for specific substrate challenges, guiding future catalyst design.
proposals.novelty: While MLC is established for activated substrates, its deliberate application to overcome severe steric hindrance in dehydrogenative coupling is unexplored. The integration of transient thiol cooperation or calcium-assisted activation, concepts pioneered in Milstein's group for other reactions, into this specific challenging transformation is a novel strategy.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Pincer Complexes for Photocatalytic Acceptorless Dehydrogenation via Thiol-Based Ligand Shuttling
proposals.motivation: Acceptorless dehydrogenation reactions are crucial for sustainable chemical synthesis and hydrogen storage, but typically require energy-intensive thermal conditions. Photocatalysis offers a pathway to drive these transformations under milder conditions using light. Merging this with Milstein's established expertise in metal-ligand cooperation, particularly the use of thiols as transient cooperative ligands, could unlock new, energy-efficient catalytic cycles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pincer complexes featuring metal-ligand cooperation can be integrated with a photosensitizer to create a dual catalytic system where light absorption drives the key dehydrogenation step, with thiol-based ligand shuttling facilitating proton and electron transfer.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize new manganese and calcium pincer complexes, designed with pendant thiol or thiolate groups, based on Milstein's prior work. These will be paired with well-established organic photocatalysts (e.g., phenothiazines or Ir/Ru polypyridyl complexes, pre-2026 standards). The system will be tested for the light-driven dehydrogenation of model substrates like formic acid, alcohols, or saturated N-heterocycles. Reaction kinetics, quantum yields, and mechanistic studies (including spectroscopic identification of key intermediates and H2 evolution quantification) will be performed to map the catalytic cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for merging metal-ligand cooperation with photocatalysis, specifically for dehydrogenation. It will provide fundamental insights into light-driven proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) processes mediated by cooperative ligands and deliver a proof-of-concept for milder, solar-powered hydrogen production from chemical feedstocks.
proposals.novelty: While photocatalytic hydrogen evolution is well-known, its application to drive the dehydrogenation step in Milstein's metal-ligand cooperative systems, utilizing thiols as transient shuttles activated by light, is unexplored and non-obvious. This moves beyond thermal catalysis and simple photosensitization to a integrated cooperative photochemical mechanism.

proposals.title: Metal–Ligand Cooperative Pincer Complexes with Bifunctional Sites for Tandem Alcohol Oxidation–Imine Formation
proposals.motivation: The direct, one-pot synthesis of imines from alcohols and amines is an atom-economical route to valuable chemical building blocks, but typically requires separate catalysts or harsh conditions for the oxidation and condensation steps. Pre-2026, Milstein's work on metal–ligand cooperation (MLC) and template catalysis, especially with thiols and pincer ligands, has demonstrated precise control over bond activation. This proposal seeks to unify these concepts to create a single, efficient catalyst for this tandem transformation under mild, base-free conditions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rationally designed pincer complex, featuring a metal center for alcohol dehydrogenation and a spatially proximate, cooperative ligand site for activating the amine and facilitating condensation, will catalyze the direct synthesis of imines from alcohols and amines in a single pot with high efficiency and selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel ruthenium and manganese pincer complexes based on pyridine- or acridine-based backbones, incorporating a secondary amine or thiol moiety on a ligand arm to serve as the cooperative site. Catalytic activity will be screened for the model reaction of benzyl alcohol with aniline. Reaction progress, intermediate trapping, and deuterium labeling studies will be used to elucidate the mechanism, distinguishing between a concerted bifunctional pathway and sequential steps. The influence of metal identity, ligand backbone, and the nature of the cooperative site (e.g., -NH vs. -SH) on activity and selectivity will be systematically evaluated.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for bifunctional earth-abundant metal catalysts that mimic the compartmentalized active sites of enzymes. It will expand the scope of MLC beyond traditional (de)hydrogenation to encompass tandem sequences, providing a mechanistic blueprint for integrating multiple reaction steps within a single catalyst framework.
proposals.novelty: While MLC is well-established for single bond activation steps, its application to orchestrate two distinct reaction types (oxidation and C–N bond formation) in a controlled, sequential manner within one catalyst is unexplored. The deliberate spatial separation of functional sites, inspired by enzyme architecture, represents a significant departure from conventional homogeneous catalyst design.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis in Reverse: Hydrogenation of Imines to Amines via a Reversible Acceptorless Dehydrogenation Platform
proposals.motivation: The development of reversible, atom-economical catalytic cycles for the interconversion of amines and imines using H2 is a fundamental challenge in sustainable chemistry. While Milstein's group has pioneered acceptorless dehydrogenation of amines to imines using pincer complexes, systematically applying the same catalysts in reverse for hydrogenation remains underexplored. Establishing such reversibility would validate the mechanistic principles of metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) and provide efficient, base-metal pathways for amine synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pincer complexes, particularly those based on earth-abundant metals like Mn or Ca and featuring cooperative thiol or amido ligands, which catalyze amine dehydrogenation, can operate in reverse to catalyze the hydrogenation of imines to amines under H2, with the reversibility governed by precise tuning of the ligand's cooperative site.
proposals.method: We will select known Mn- and Ca-based pincer catalysts from Milstein's pre-2026 work that are effective in amine dehydrogenation. Their activity will be tested in reverse for the hydrogenation of a series of imines under H2 (1-10 bar) in various solvents, monitoring conversion via NMR/GC. Detailed mechanistic studies, including kinetic profiling, deuterium labeling (H2/D2), and in-situ spectroscopic analysis (IR, NMR), will be conducted to identify the role of the ligand's cooperative site (e.g., S-H or N-H bond formation/cleavage) in the reverse hydrogenation step and compare it to the forward dehydrogenation pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct experimental demonstration of full reversibility in MLC-catalyzed amine/imine interconversion, a rare achievement. It will establish structure-activity relationships for designing bidirectional catalysts and deliver new, efficient protocols for imine hydrogenation using earth-abundant, base-free catalytic systems derived from a dehydrogenation platform.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in intentionally testing the inverse reaction with the *same* catalysts used for dehydrogenation, focusing on the mechanistic symmetry of the MLC step, rather than developing a new catalyst solely for hydrogenation. This directly probes the fundamental reversibility of these cooperative systems.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Pincer Catalysts in Tandem Systems: Merging Hydrogenation and C–C Bond Formation for One-Pot Synthesis of Functionalized Amines
proposals.motivation: The synthesis of complex molecules often requires multiple isolation and purification steps, reducing efficiency and atom economy. Tandem catalysis, where multiple catalytic cycles operate in one pot, offers a powerful solution. However, integrating distinct catalytic cycles, particularly those involving metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), remains a significant challenge due to potential catalyst incompatibility and interference.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that well-defined MLC catalysts based on Milstein's pincer platforms, particularly those with thiol or amine-based cooperative ligands, can be effectively integrated with other catalytic cycles (e.g., base-metal-catalyzed C–C coupling) in a single pot without decomposition, enabling sequential transformations via orthogonal activation modes.
proposals.method: We will design a one-pot system where an MLC catalyst (e.g., a Mn or Fe pincer complex with a thiolate arm) first performs a selective hydrogenation (e.g., of a nitrile to an imine or an unsaturated ester). The resulting intermediate will then be engaged in a second catalytic cycle, such as a Mn- or Ca-catalyzed C–C bond formation (e.g., hydrocyanation or nitrile addition) recently developed in the group. Key to the method is systematic screening of reaction conditions (order of addition, solvent, temperature) and catalyst pairing to ensure compatibility, leveraging the unique activation pathways (e.g., ligand aromatization/dearomatization, thiol cooperation) of the MLC catalyst that are orthogonal to the mechanisms of the secondary catalyst.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish design principles for integrating MLC catalysts into complex reaction networks, moving beyond single-substrate transformations. It will deliver novel, streamlined synthetic routes to valuable nitrogen-containing compounds. The project will demonstrate how the mechanistic nuances of MLC can be exploited for temporal control in multi-catalytic systems.
proposals.novelty: While MLC catalysts are well-studied for individual (de)hydrogenation steps, their deliberate integration with other catalytic cycles in a designed tandem sequence is largely unexplored. The novelty lies in using the specific ligand-based activation modes of Milstein-type complexes as a chemically orthogonal "module" within a one-pot synthetic sequence.

proposals.title: Earth-Abundant Manganese Pincer Complexes with Cooperative Thiol Ligands for the Catalytic Hydroamination of Unactivated Alkenes
proposals.motivation: The development of catalytic hydroamination using earth-abundant metals remains a significant challenge in sustainable chemistry, as current efficient methods often rely on precious metals like palladium, platinum, or iridium. This transformation is crucial for the atom-economical synthesis of valuable amines from simple olefin and amine feedstocks. Milstein's recent work on metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly with thiols as transient ligands and with calcium, provides a powerful conceptual framework to address this challenge with base metals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that manganese pincer complexes, incorporating a designed thiol/thiolate ligand framework, will facilitate the catalytic hydroamination of unactivated alkenes via a mechanism involving reversible thiol coordination and metal-ligand cooperative activation of both the N-H and C=C bonds.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Mn(I) pincer complexes featuring PNP and PNN backbones with pendant thiol/thioether arms, inspired by Milstein's recent acceleration/inhibition studies. Catalytic screening will focus on the direct coupling of aliphatic amines with unactivated terminal alkenes (e.g., 1-octene) under base-free conditions. Reaction progress will be monitored by GC and NMR, with mechanistic interrogation via stoichiometric reactions, deuterium labeling, and in-situ spectroscopic studies (IR, NMR) to identify key intermediates and validate the proposed thiol-assisted pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general manganese-catalyzed system for intermolecular hydroamination of unactivated alkenes, directly replacing precious metals. It will provide fundamental insights into how transient, cooperative thiol ligands can modulate reactivity in base-metal catalysis, expanding the toolbox of MLC beyond classical aromatization/dearomatization or classical heteroatom donors.
proposals.novelty: While manganese catalysis and thiol cooperation are known individually, their combined application to the mechanistically distinct challenge of alkene hydroamination is unexplored. This moves beyond Milstein's prior use of thiols in (de)hydrogenation to a fundamentally different bond-forming reaction, leveraging thiol dynamics for C-N coupling.

proposals.title: Ambient-Pressure Catalytic Amination of Alcohols via Metal–Ligand Cooperation with a Thiol-Functionalized Pincer Complex
proposals.motivation: Catalytic amination using ammonia is a crucial route to primary amines, but state-of-the-art homogeneous systems typically require high-pressure NH3 gas (10–50 bar), posing significant safety and practical limitations for laboratory and industrial synthesis. Developing efficient catalysts that operate under ambient pressure (1 bar NH3) would greatly enhance the utility and accessibility of this transformation. This aligns with the broader push in sustainable chemistry to replace harsh conditions with selective, earth-abundant metal catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a manganese or calcium pincer complex featuring a thiol-functionalized ligand, operating via metal–ligand cooperative mechanisms, can activate ammonia at ambient pressure and facilitate its addition to alcohols or activated alkenes through a dehydrogenation–condensation–hydrogenation sequence.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of Mn(I) and Ca(II) pincer complexes with pendant thiol or thiolate groups on the ligand backbone, inspired by Milstein's recent work on thiols as transient cooperative ligands. These complexes will be screened in the amination of benzyl alcohol and other substrates using 1 atm of NH3 gas in a sealed vessel, with reaction progress monitored by GC and NMR. Key parameters include base identity, temperature (80–120°C), and solvent effects. Mechanistic studies will involve isolation of intermediates, deuterium labeling, and in-situ spectroscopic analysis to elucidate the role of the thiol in NH3 activation and hydrogen transfer.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first homogeneous catalyst system for the direct amination of alcohols with ammonia at ambient pressure, significantly lowering the practical barrier for primary amine synthesis. It will provide fundamental insights into how metal–ligand cooperation, particularly with thiols, can be harnessed to activate small molecules like NH3 under mild conditions, extending the principles of cooperative catalysis to a new challenging transformation.
proposals.novelty: While metal–ligand cooperation is established for (de)hydrogenation, its application to achieve ambient-pressure ammonia activation and coupling is unexplored. The strategic use of a thiol-based cooperative ligand, rather than the traditional aromatization/dearomatization or amine/amide platforms, to facilitate NH3 incorporation is a novel and non-obvious design inspired by recent inhibition/acceleration effects observed in Milstein's group.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis with Thiolate Pincer Complexes: Broadening the Scope for the Guerbet-Type Coupling of Alcohols and Amines
proposals.motivation: The catalytic upgrading of simple alcohols and amines via borrowing-hydrogen or Guerbet-type reactions is a pivotal atom-economical strategy for synthesizing higher-value chemicals from feedstocks. While Milstein's group has recently demonstrated that thiols can act as transient cooperative ligands to dramatically accelerate (de)hydrogenation steps in specific systems, the generality of this effect across diverse alcohol and amine substrates remains unexplored. Extending this catalytic platform to a wider substrate scope would unlock efficient routes to valuable branched alcohols, diamines, and N-alkylated amines, advancing sustainable synthetic methodologies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the unique metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) facilitated by thiolate groups in pincer complexes provides a general and tunable platform for the efficient coupling of a broad range of primary and secondary alcohols with ammonia surrogates (e.g., NH3, ammonium salts, or primary amines), overcoming limitations in substrate compatibility and selectivity seen with traditional catalysts.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and screen a library of Mn(I) and Ca(II) pincer complexes featuring thiolate or thioether arms, building on pre-2026 precedents. These catalysts will be tested in the coupling of diverse alcohols (e.g., linear and branched C2-C8 alkanols, benzyl alcohol derivatives, and diols) with various nitrogen nucleophiles under base-free conditions. Reaction optimization will focus on temperature, pressure, and catalyst loading, with mechanistic studies (kinetic isotope effects, NMR spectroscopy, and isolation of proposed intermediates) to elucidate the role of the thiol in different substrate classes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first general catalytic system using thiolate MLC for C–C and C–N bond formation via alcohol coupling, significantly expanding the synthetic toolbox. It will provide fundamental insights into how transient ligand participation can be harnessed to control selectivity and activity across challenging substrate pairs, moving beyond model reactions to practical synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While thiol acceleration has been shown for hydrogenation/dehydrogenation of specific substrates, its application to govern the selectivity and rate of the multi-step cascade involved in alcohol-amine coupling is unprecedented. The systematic exploration of earth-abundant metal (Mn, Ca) catalysts with thiolate ligands for this broad substrate scope represents a distinct departure from noble-metal-based systems.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Control of Nucleophilicity in Pincer Complexes for the Selective Catalytic Hydrogenation of Nitriles to Primary Amines
proposals.motivation: The catalytic hydrogenation of nitriles is a crucial route to amines, valuable building blocks in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals. However, state-of-the-art catalysts (pre-2026) often suffer from poor selectivity, yielding mixtures of primary, secondary, and tertiary amines due to the over-reaction of the primary amine product with intermediate imines. Achieving high selectivity for the primary amine remains a significant challenge in sustainable synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically integrating thiol-based cooperative ligands into earth-abundant metal (e.g., Mn, Ca) pincer complexes, we can modulate the catalyst's electronic and steric environment to kinetically favor the release of the primary amine product and disfavor its subsequent condensation with reactive intermediates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of pincer ligands featuring pendant thiol/thiolate groups, known for their dynamic metal-ligand cooperation, and complex them with Mn(I) and Ca(II). These complexes will be screened in the hydrogenation of benchmark nitriles under base-free conditions. Reaction progress and selectivity will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR. We will systematically vary the steric bulk near the metal center and the electronics of the thiol arm, correlating structure with selectivity data. Mechanistic studies, including kinetics and deuterium labeling, will probe the role of the thiol in stabilizing key intermediates and facilitating product dissociation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of selective, base-free catalysts for primary amine synthesis, moving beyond precious metals. It will provide fundamental insights into how dynamic, cooperative ligands can be engineered to control product selectivity in multi-step catalytic cycles, a principle applicable beyond hydrogenation.
proposals.novelty: While thiol cooperation is known to accelerate (de)hydrogenation, its deliberate application to *inhibit* a specific, undesired reaction pathway (secondary amine formation) by controlling product release is a novel strategy for selectivity engineering in nitrile hydrogenation.

proposals.title: Heterogenization of Metal–Ligand Cooperative Pincer Catalysts via Thiolate Anchoring to Mesoporous Supports for Recyclable Hydrogenation Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Homogeneous catalysts based on metal–ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly Milstein's pincer complexes, offer exceptional activity and selectivity in hydrogenation and related reactions. However, their industrial application is hampered by difficulties in catalyst separation and recycling. Heterogenization of such sophisticated molecular catalysts remains a significant challenge, as it must preserve the crucial MLC mechanism and active site integrity while enabling practical recovery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the thiolate groups, which have been shown to act as transient cooperative ligands in Milstein's homogeneous systems, can be utilized as permanent, covalent anchors to immobilize analogous pincer complexes onto solid supports without compromising the metal–ligand cooperative framework essential for catalysis.
proposals.method: We will synthesize modified pincer ligands with a terminal thiol or protected thiol group on the ligand backbone. These will be used to prepare Mn(I) and Ca(II) complexes analogous to Milstein's recent homogeneous catalysts. These thiol-functionalized complexes will be covalently grafted onto mesoporous silica (e.g., SBA-15) and metal-oxide supports via stable thiolate linkages. The resulting materials will be characterized by XRD, XPS, solid-state NMR, and IR spectroscopy. Their catalytic performance will be evaluated in benchmark hydrogenation reactions (e.g., of imines, quinoline, and alkenes) under base-free conditions, with a focus on activity, stability, and recyclability over multiple cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable strategy for heterogenizing sophisticated MLC catalysts, bridging the gap between homogeneous precision and heterogeneous practicality. It will yield fundamental insights into how immobilization affects cooperative ligand behavior and catalytic cycles. The development of a robust, recyclable catalyst system would advance sustainable catalysis by reducing metal waste and streamlining processes.
proposals.novelty: While heterogenization of pincer complexes is known, exploiting the intrinsic thiolate chemistry of the catalyst itself as the anchoring point is novel. This approach is designed to minimally perturb the active site geometry and electronic structure, aiming to retain the unique acceleration/inhibition effects and base-free operation characteristic of Milstein's homogeneous thiolate-cooperative systems.

proposals.title: Computational Design of Cooperative Pincer Ligands for Earth-Abundant Metal Catalysis: A DFT Study on Thiolate and Amide Donor Effects in Hydrogenation
proposals.motivation: The Milstein group's recent work has established metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly with thiols and other heteroatom donors, as a powerful strategy for activating small molecules and enabling catalysis with earth-abundant metals like manganese and calcium. However, the rational design of new cooperative ligands remains largely empirical. A predictive computational framework is needed to understand how subtle ligand modifications influence key catalytic steps, such as H2 activation and substrate insertion, to accelerate the discovery of more active and selective catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculations can accurately predict the thermodynamic and kinetic parameters of catalytic cycles involving pincer complexes with cooperative thiolate/amide donors, and that these parameters correlate directly with experimentally observed activity and selectivity in hydrogenation reactions.
proposals.method: We will employ DFT calculations (using functionals like B3LYP-D3 with def2-TZVP basis sets) to model the full catalytic cycles for imine and alkene hydrogenation using Mn and Ca pincer complexes. The study will systematically vary the donor atom (S, NH, O), backbone aromaticity, and substituent electronic properties in the ligand framework. Key transition states for H2 heterolysis and hydride transfer will be located and characterized. The computational workflow will be automated using scripts to calculate descriptors like hydricity, proton affinity, and steric maps, correlating them with activation barriers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, structure-activity relationship for cooperative pincer ligands, moving ligand design from intuition to prediction. It will deliver a set of design principles for tuning catalyst performance and propose 2-3 novel ligand scaffolds with predicted superior activity for experimental validation by the Milstein group.
proposals.novelty: While DFT is commonly used to study known catalysts, its proactive application to design new cooperative ligands for earth-abundant metals, specifically focusing on the dynamic role of thiols and amides in MLC, is a novel and non-obvious extension of the group's recent experimental discoveries.

proposals.title: pH-Responsive Hydrogen Acceptors in Metal–Ligand Cooperative Catalysis: Dynamic Control of (De)Hydrogenation Equilibria
proposals.motivation: In acceptorless dehydrogenation and hydrogenation catalysis, achieving high conversions often requires elevated temperatures to drive unfavorable equilibria. While additives like bases can accelerate metal–ligand cooperative steps, and stoichiometric hydrogen acceptors can shift equilibria, these strategies are typically static and can lead to side reactions or complicate product isolation. A system where the additive dynamically controls the acceptor's function would offer unprecedented temporal control over reaction pathways, enabling milder conditions and higher selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating pH-modifying additives with hydrogen acceptors whose binding affinity or reactivity is pH-dependent, we can create a synergistic, temporally controlled catalytic system. This will allow for sequential optimization of the activation and completion stages of (de)hydrogenation reactions, leading to enhanced performance under milder conditions.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of thiol-based and other heteroatom-functionalized pincer ligands for Mn and Ca complexes, inspired by Milstein's recent work on thiols as transient cooperative ligands and calcium–ligand cooperation. These will be paired with pH-sensitive hydrogen acceptors, such as α,β-unsaturated carbonyls or nitriles whose Michael acceptor activity is modulated by protonation state. Catalytic testing will focus on model reactions like imine hydrogenation and alcohol dehydrogenation, systematically varying pH via weak organic acids/bases to correlate acceptor efficiency and catalytic rate. In-situ NMR and UV-Vis spectroscopy will monitor the protonation state of the acceptor and metal–hydride intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for controlling catalytic equilibria via dynamic, additive-responsive systems, moving beyond static acceptor use. It will provide fundamental insights into how proton-coupled electron transfer and substrate binding can be co-managed. The resulting protocols should deliver more efficient, lower-temperature (de)hydrogenation catalysis with broad applicability in organic synthesis.
proposals.novelty: While pH effects and hydrogen acceptors are known individually, their deliberate fusion into a single, cooperatively controlled system is unexplored. The novelty lies in using the pH modifier not just as a co-catalyst but as a direct regulator of the hydrogen acceptor's mode of action, creating a feedback loop within Milstein's metal–ligand cooperation framework.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis for the Oxidative Cross-Coupling of Primary Alcohols to Asymmetric Esters
proposals.motivation: The synthesis of unsymmetrical esters is a fundamental transformation in organic synthesis, yet current methods typically rely on pre-activated acid derivatives (e.g., acid chlorides, anhydrides) or require stoichiometric coupling agents, generating significant waste. A direct, catalytic cross-coupling of two distinct alcohols via dehydrogenative coupling represents a highly atom-economical and step-efficient alternative. However, achieving high selectivity for the cross-product over the two possible symmetric esters (homo-coupling) remains a major, unsolved challenge in catalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bifunctional pincer-ligand complex, operating via metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) with a thiol/thiolate moiety, can orchestrate the sequential, rate-differentiated activation of two different alcohols, enabling high selectivity for the asymmetric ester via controlled dehydrogenation and acyl transfer.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize Mn(I) and Ca(II) pincer complexes featuring a thiol/thiolate cooperative site, inspired by Milstein's recent work on thiol-mediated (de)hydrogenation. Catalytic screening will employ benzyl alcohol and a slower-oxidizing aliphatic alcohol (e.g., 1-hexanol) with N2O or a ketone as a hydrogen acceptor. Kinetic studies (initial rates, competition experiments) will probe the relative rates of dehydrogenation for each alcohol. The catalyst's ability to reversibly bind the first alcohol as an alkoxide, dehydrogenate it to an aldehyde (bound as an acyl), and then regulate the nucleophilic attack by the second alcohol via the thiolate site will be investigated through in-situ spectroscopy and DFT calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, waste-minimized catalytic methodology for C–O bond formation. It will provide fundamental insights into how MLC, particularly with thiols, can be used to control reaction sequences and selectivity in multi-substrate transformations, extending the principles of 'template catalysis' to cross-coupling. The development of earth-abundant metal catalysts (Mn, Ca) for this challenging transformation is a significant goal.
proposals.novelty: While alcohol dehydrogenation to esters (Tishchenko-type reactions) is known, achieving high cross-selectivity from two different alcohols using a single, rationally designed catalyst system is unprecedented. The novelty lies in leveraging transient thiol cooperation not just for acceleration, but for kinetic differentiation and selective acyl transfer between two competing substrates.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis for Functional Group-Directed Cross-Esterification of Polyfunctional Alcohols
proposals.motivation: The direct, catalytic cross-esterification of two distinct alcohols is a highly desirable yet challenging atom-economical route to asymmetric esters, especially from complex, polyfunctional building blocks common in pharmaceuticals and natural products. Current methods often require pre-activation, lack selectivity, or fail in the presence of sensitive functional groups. A catalytic strategy that not only tolerates but utilizes these functional groups to guide selectivity would represent a paradigm shift in ester synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pincer-ligand metal complexes, operating via metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), can be designed to selectively bind and activate one alcohol partner over another based on specific functional group interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding with thiols, coordination with amines), thereby directing the cross-esterification pathway with high selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Mn, Fe, and Ca complexes bearing pincer ligands with cooperative sites (e.g., thiols, amides, phenols) inspired by Milstein's recent work. These catalysts will be screened in the cross-esterification of model diols with alcohols bearing orthogonal functional groups (e.g., amino-alcohols, thio-alcohols). Reaction progress and selectivity will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR. Mechanistic studies, including kinetics, isotopic labeling (D2O, 18O), and in-situ spectroscopy, will probe the role of functional group pre-coordination and MLC in the catalytic cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first catalytic system for functional group-directed cross-esterification, moving beyond simple tolerance to active substrate guidance. It will provide fundamental insights into how cooperative ligand sites can interact with substrate functionality to control selectivity in bond-forming reactions, expanding the toolbox of MLC.
proposals.novelty: While MLC is established for (de)hydrogenation, its application to direct esterification is rare, and the concept of using substrate functional groups as directing handles for selectivity in this context is unprecedented, turning traditional incompatibilities into strategic advantages.

proposals.title: Light-Driven, Acceptor-Free Hydrogenation via Metal-Ligand Cooperative Photoredox Shuttles
proposals.motivation: Conventional (de)hydrogenation catalysis often requires stoichiometric sacrificial oxidants or hydrogen acceptors, generating waste and complicating reaction design. While Milstein's work on metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) has enabled efficient hydrogen transfer, many reactions remain thermodynamically limited. A general, catalytic method to shift these equilibria without a net stoichiometric reagent would be transformative for sustainable synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a photoredox cycle with a metal-ligand cooperative platform can create a regenerable hydrogen shuttle, using light energy to drive hydrogen away from a substrate and irreversibly toward product formation, thereby overcoming thermodynamic limitations in acceptor-free hydrogenation.
proposals.method: We will design pincer-ligated Mn or Ca complexes where the ligand (e.g., a thiol or amine) acts as a transient hydrogen carrier. This complex will be coupled with a strong photoreductant (e.g., an Ir(III) polypyridyl complex). Under irradiation, the photoreductant will deliver an electron to the metal center, promoting ligand-based hydrogen abstraction from a substrate (e.g., an alcohol). The resulting metal-hydride will then undergo a fast, irreversible proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) to a less-activated, unsaturated substrate (e.g., a ketone or imine), completing the hydrogenation and regenerating the initial catalyst states. Key studies will involve spectroscopic identification of PCET intermediates and quantum yield measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for photochemically driven hydrogenation that is not reliant on sacrificial donors/acceptors, directly expanding the scope of Milstein's MLC and thiol-cooperation concepts. It will provide a mechanistic blueprint for using light to pump hydrogen against a concentration gradient, enabling new transformations under mild conditions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of photoredox catalysis with metal-ligand cooperation to create a closed, catalytic cycle for hydrogen shuttling, moving beyond stoichiometric acceptors or simple photocatalytic hydrogenation where the photocatalyst directly interacts with the substrate.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Screening of Metal-Ligand Cooperative Pincer Catalysts for the Selective Dehydrogenation of Secondary Alcohols
proposals.motivation: The catalytic dehydrogenation of secondary alcohols to ketones is a pivotal, atom-economical oxidation step in organic synthesis and potential hydrogen storage cycles. However, achieving high selectivity, especially for unsymmetrical diols or alcohols bearing sensitive functional groups, remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026, Milstein's work has established metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) with pincer ligands as a powerful paradigm for (de)hydrogenation, but systematic exploration of catalyst-substrate selectivity relationships is labor-intensive and slow.
proposals.hypothesis: That high-throughput experimentation (HTE) will rapidly reveal how systematic variations in the steric and electronic properties of MLC pincer catalysts—particularly those featuring emerging ligand motifs like thiols or earth-abundant metals—govern selectivity in the dehydrogenation of diverse secondary alcohol substrates.
proposals.method: We will synthesize focused libraries of Mn, Fe, and Ca pincer complexes, varying the donor groups (e.g., P, N, S), backbone rigidity, and flanking aryl substituents. These catalyst libraries will be screened in parallel using an automated workstation in 96-well microtiter plates against a diverse substrate array including aliphatic, benzylic, sterically hindered, and heteroatom-functionalized secondary alcohols. Reaction progress and selectivity will be monitored in situ via high-throughput GC-MS or HPLC-UV analysis. Data will be analyzed to construct catalyst performance maps, correlating ligand/metal features with activity and chemoselectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive dataset linking MLC pincer catalyst structure to selectivity in alcohol dehydrogenation, directly guiding the rational design of superior catalysts. It will establish a general HTE workflow for accelerating discovery in MLC catalysis and identify lead catalysts for challenging, previously inaccessible selective transformations.
