proposals.novelty: The fusion of Milstein's deep expertise in MLC and pincer chemistry with miniaturized, parallelized screening represents a novel methodological advance for the field. This approach moves beyond studying single catalysts on single substrates to systematically mapping the multi-dimensional chemical space of catalyst-substrate interactions in dehydrogenation.

proposals.title: Metal–Ligand Cooperation with Thiolate Pincer Complexes for the Base-Free, Acceptories Dehydrogenation of Secondary Alcohols
proposals.motivation: The catalytic dehydrogenation of secondary alcohols to ketones is a fundamental and atom-economical oxidation reaction, crucial for fine chemical synthesis. State-of-the-art homogeneous catalysts, however, typically require strong bases, sacrificial hydrogen acceptors, or precious metals, which complicate processes and increase costs. Developing efficient, base-free, and acceptorless systems using earth-abundant metals remains a significant challenge in sustainable catalysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the metal–ligand cooperation (MLC) paradigm, particularly the transient thiol/thiolate cooperation recently demonstrated by the Milstein group for (de)hydrogenation, can be effectively harnessed to facilitate the kinetically challenging β-hydride elimination step in secondary alcohol dehydrogenation without an external base or acceptor.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of ruthenium and manganese pincer complexes featuring thiol/thiolate cooperative sites, such as PNS and SNS frameworks. These catalysts will be screened in the acceptorless dehydrogenation of model secondary alcohols (e.g., 1-phenylethanol) under inert atmosphere, monitoring H2 evolution by gas chromatography. Reaction parameters (temperature, solvent, catalyst loading) will be optimized. Mechanistic studies, including kinetic analysis, deuterium labeling, and isolation of potential intermediates, will be conducted to elucidate the role of the thiol/thiolate moiety in the catalytic cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, operationally simple catalytic method for ketone synthesis using earth-abundant metals under benign conditions. It will provide fundamental insights into how transient ligand cooperation can be leveraged for challenging bond activations, expanding the scope of MLC beyond established substrate classes like nitriles and imines.
proposals.novelty: While MLC pincer complexes are known for hydrogenation and acceptorless dehydrogenation of alcohols to esters (via tandem dehydrogenation–esterification), their direct application to the thermodynamically less favored, base-free, acceptorless dehydrogenation of secondary alcohols to ketones is unexplored. The specific use of thiol-based transient cooperation for this transformation is a novel design principle.

proposals.title: Metal–Ligand Cooperative Pincer Complexes in Photoredox-Catalyzed Dehydrogenative Lactonization via Hydrogen Atom Transfer
proposals.motivation: Lactones are crucial structural motifs in pharmaceuticals and polymers, yet their synthesis from readily available diols or hydroxy acids often requires stoichiometric, toxic oxidants or harsh conditions, limiting functional group tolerance and selectivity. Pre-2026 advances in photoredox catalysis and hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) have enabled mild C–H functionalization, but their merger for direct, catalytic dehydrogenative cyclization remains underdeveloped, especially for complex polyol substrates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a synergistic system combining a photoredox catalyst with a HAT co-catalyst based on a metal–ligand cooperative pincer platform will enable efficient, selective, and base-free lactonization under visible light, where the pincer complex acts as a tunable HAT mediator, controlling selectivity via its ligand framework.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a library of earth-abundant manganese and calcium pincer complexes, inspired by Milstein's recent work, featuring thiol or amide cooperative ligands known to facilitate H2 and substrate activation. These will be screened as HAT co-catalysts paired with established photoredox catalysts (e.g., Ir(ppy)3 or organic dyes) under blue LED irradiation. The reaction will be tested on diverse diols and hydroxy acids, with mechanistic studies (kinetics, quenching, EPR) probing the role of metal–ligand cooperation in the key hydrogen abstraction and cyclization steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, mild catalytic method for lactone synthesis with high atom economy and functional group compatibility. It will significantly advance the understanding of how metal–ligand cooperative complexes can function as selective HAT mediators in photoredox cascades, bridging the fields of coordination chemistry and photocatalysis.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of Milstein's cooperative pincer complexes, typically used in (de)hydrogenation, as designed HAT catalysts in a photoredox manifold for lactonization is unprecedented. This moves beyond simple thiyl radical HAT to exploit tunable metal–ligand motifs for controlling reactivity and selectivity in radical cyclizations.

proposals.title: Manganese-Catalyzed Dehydrogenative Lactonization of Diols and Hydroxy Acids via Metal–Ligand Cooperation
proposals.motivation: Lactones are crucial structural motifs in pharmaceuticals, fragrances, and polymers. Traditional synthesis often relies on stoichiometric oxidants or harsh acidic conditions, generating significant waste. Catalytic dehydrogenative coupling offers a direct, atom-economical, and potentially base-free route from readily available diols or hydroxy acids, aligning with green chemistry principles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Milstein-type pincer complexes, particularly earth-abundant manganese catalysts operating via metal–ligand cooperation, can mediate the dehydrogenative coupling of 1,4-, 1,5-, and 1,6-diols (or corresponding hydroxy acids) to form γ-, δ-, and ε-lactones, respectively, with H2 as the sole byproduct.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and screen a library of Mn(I) and Ru(II) pincer complexes (e.g., PNP, PNN, SNS types) for activity. The catalytic protocol will involve heating substrate diols (e.g., 1,4-butanediol) or hydroxy acids (e.g., 4-hydroxybutyric acid) in a suitable solvent (e.g., toluene or dioxane) under inert atmosphere, monitoring H2 evolution. Key mechanistic studies will include deuterium labeling, isolation of potential catalyst-substrate intermediates, and kinetic analysis to probe the role of ligand-based thiol/thiolate or amine/amide cooperation in the dual alcohol dehydrogenation and C–O bond-forming steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, sustainable catalytic method for lactone synthesis, expanding the scope of acceptorless dehydrogenative coupling. It will provide fundamental insights into how metal–ligand cooperative platforms can facilitate challenging intramolecular C–O bond formations, potentially uncovering new modes of template catalysis or transient ligand participation.
proposals.novelty: While Milstein's systems have excelled in (de)hydrogenation and N2O activation, their direct application to intramolecular lactonization via dehydrogenation is unexplored. The proposed use of thiol-based or calcium-ligand cooperative systems to potentially accelerate the key cyclization step represents a novel extension of transient cooperative ligand concepts.

proposals.title: Visible-Light-Driven Dehydrogenation via Metal–Ligand Cooperative Pincer Complexes Merged with Photoredox Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Milstein's pre-2026 work established metal–ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly with thiols, as a powerful paradigm for (de)hydrogenation. However, these thermal processes often require elevated temperatures or strong bases. Photoredox catalysis offers a complementary pathway to initiate challenging bond activations under mild conditions using light energy. Merging these fields could unlock new, energy-efficient catalytic cycles for dehydrogenation, a fundamental step in sustainable chemical synthesis and hydrogen storage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a photoactive moiety into a pincer ligand framework, or coupling a conventional MLC catalyst with a photoredox sensitizer, will enable visible-light-driven dehydrogenation of substrates like alcohols or amines at room temperature without exogenous bases, via light-induced electron transfer processes that facilitate key hydride or proton transfer steps.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel ruthenium and manganese pincer complexes featuring ligands with integrated photoactive units (e.g., phenanthroline, diaryl ketones). These will be screened alongside dual-catalytic systems pairing established Milstein-type MLC catalysts (e.g., Ru-PNN thiol) with iridium or organic photoredox co-catalysts. Reactions will be performed under blue LED irradiation, monitoring H2 evolution and product formation via GC and NMR. Mechanistic studies will include Stern-Volmer quenching, EPR, and DFT calculations to map the photo-initiated pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new subfield combining MLC and photoredox catalysis, demonstrating a general strategy for mild, base-free bond activation. It will provide fundamental insights into photo-triggered proton-coupled electron transfer (PCET) processes within cooperative ligand frameworks and deliver practical catalytic systems for sustainable dehydrogenation transformations.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox catalysis is well-established, its application to accelerate or enable MLC-based dehydrogenation cycles, particularly using the transient thiol cooperation mechanism pioneered by Milstein, is unexplored. The design of pincer complexes where the ligand itself acts as both a cooperative site and a light-harvesting antenna is a novel architectural concept.

proposals.title: Manganese Pincer Complexes with Cooperative Thiolate/Amine Ligands for Base-Free, Concerted Hydrogenation of Polar Bonds
proposals.motivation: The development of sustainable, base-free hydrogenation catalysts using earth-abundant metals remains a significant challenge. Milstein's recent work on metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) with thiols as transient ligands and calcium-ligand cooperation for N2O/H2 activation demonstrates the power of bifunctional design. Adapting these principles to create enzyme-inspired, concerted proton-hydride transfer pathways could unlock new reactivity for challenging substrates without stoichiometric additives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing manganese pincer complexes featuring a proton-responsive secondary amine site in concert with a metal-coordinated thiolate will create a bifunctional catalyst capable of concerted, base-free hydrogenation of imines and nitriles via a metal-hydride/thiol-proton shuttle mechanism.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel Mn(I) pincer complexes based on a PNP scaffold, where the central amine is replaced by a thioether-appended secondary amine, allowing for in-situ deprotonation to a thiolate. Catalytic activity will be tested in the hydrogenation of N-benzylideneaniline and benzonitrile under base-free conditions. Reaction kinetics, deuterium labeling studies, and in-situ NMR/IR spectroscopy will be used to probe the mechanism, specifically looking for evidence of a simultaneous hydride transfer from Mn-H and proton transfer from the thiol group in the rate-determining step.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of bifunctional Mn catalysts operating via a novel thiolate/amine cooperative pathway, expanding the toolbox for base-free hydrogenation. It will provide fundamental mechanistic insights into how transient ligand cooperation can be engineered for concerted bond activation, bridging concepts from enzymatic catalysis and homogeneous transition metal catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While MLC with aromatization/dearomatization or oxygen-based sites is established, the integration of a thiol(thiolate) redox couple with a proton-responsive amine in a single Mn pincer complex for concerted catalysis is unexplored. This design directly merges insights from Milstein's recent work on thiol acceleration and calcium-ligand cooperation into a first-row transition metal system.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperative Catalysis for the Hydrogenation of Esters to Alcohols Using Pincer Complexes
proposals.motivation: The catalytic hydrogenation of esters to alcohols is a pivotal transformation for sustainable chemistry, offering a green route to valuable chemicals from biomass-derived feedstocks. However, state-of-the-art catalysts often rely on expensive precious metals (e.g., Ru, Ir) and strong bases, raising cost and environmental concerns. Developing efficient, base-free catalysts based on earth-abundant metals remains a significant challenge in pre-2026 catalysis research.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pincer complexes featuring metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly those with thiol or calcium-based cooperative ligands as demonstrated in Milstein's recent work, can be adapted to catalyze the challenging hydrogenation of unactivated esters to alcohols under base-free conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Mn, Fe, and Ca pincer complexes with thiolate, alkoxide, and amide cooperative ligands, inspired by recent pre-2026 reports from the Milstein group. These complexes will be screened in the hydrogenation of benchmark esters (e.g., ethyl acetate, methyl benzoate) under varying pressures of H2 and temperatures. Reaction progress will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR. Mechanistic studies, including kinetic analysis, deuterium labeling, and isolation of potential intermediates, will be conducted to elucidate the role of MLC in the proposed outer-sphere hydride transfer and C-O bond cleavage steps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, base-free catalytic paradigm for ester hydrogenation using earth-abundant metals, directly contributing to green chemistry goals. It will expand the scope of metal-ligand cooperation to a challenging bond activation, providing fundamental mechanistic insights into reversible dehydrogenation/hydrogenation cycles.
proposals.novelty: While MLC pincer complexes are known for alcohol dehydrogenation, their application to the reverse—ester hydrogenation—is underexplored. The novelty lies in deliberately applying catalysts designed for dehydrogenation to their 'inverse' problem, specifically leveraging transient thiol cooperation or hard Lewis acid (Ca)/ligand motifs for this demanding reduction.

proposals.title: Integrating Metal-Ligand Cooperative Pincer Complexes for a Catalytic, Two-Stage Water Splitting Cycle
proposals.motivation: Artificial photosynthesis, the splitting of water into H2 and O2 using sunlight, remains a grand challenge for sustainable energy. Pre-2026 work, including Milstein's own, has demonstrated the separate thermal liberation of H2 from water and light-induced O2 liberation using designed pincer complexes, but these steps have not been integrated into a single, efficient, and truly catalytic cycle. Closing this cycle is the critical next step for practical solar fuel generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a bifunctional catalytic system, leveraging metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) and the transient ligand effects recently explored with thiols, can efficiently couple the thermal H2 evolution and photo-induced O2 evolution steps into a closed, catalytic water-splitting cycle without sacrificial agents.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize new pincer complexes based on earth-abundant metals (Mn, Fe, Ca), incorporating both redox-active and chemically cooperative ligands (e.g., pyridine-based with pendant thiol or amine groups). The thermal H2 evolution step will be optimized using these complexes via MLC pathways. Subsequently, we will integrate these complexes with known water oxidation catalysts (e.g., Ru-polypyridyl or first-row metal complexes) into a biphasic or molecular dyad system. The full cycle will be tested in a photochemical reactor, using visible light to drive the O2-evolving half, with the MLC complex mediating H+ reduction and shuttling electrons between the half-reactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a fully catalytic, molecular water-splitting cycle driven by visible light, moving beyond stepwise stoichiometric reactions. It will advance the understanding of how MLC and transient ligand effects can be harnessed to couple multi-electron proton-transfer and light-absorption events, a key requirement for artificial photosynthesis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate integration of Milstein's established MLC chemistry for H2 release with a photo-driven O2 evolution step within a single system, using the principles of transient ligand participation (e.g., thiols) to potentially accelerate and synchronize the coupled redox processes, which has not been achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperation via Aromatization-Dearomatization for the Catalytic Hydrofunctionalization of Carbonyls and Imines with Thiols
proposals.motivation: The Milstein group's Metal-Ligand Cooperation (MLC) via aromatization-dearomatization of pincer complexes has revolutionized catalytic (de)hydrogenation of alcohols and amines. However, its application to other fundamental substrate classes, particularly for bond-forming reactions beyond hydrogenation, remains largely unexplored. Extending this powerful paradigm to activate carbonyls and imines for direct hydrofunctionalization with thiols would unlock novel, atom-economical routes to valuable thioethers, which are important motifs in pharmaceuticals and materials.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pyridine-based PNP and PNN pincer complexes of Mn, Fe, and Ru, known for reversible aromatization-dearomatization, can activate C=O and C=N bonds via ligand dearomatization, enabling the catalytic addition of S-H bonds across these unsaturated linkages.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Mn(I), Fe(II), and Ru(II) pincer complexes with tunable electronics and sterics. Their catalytic activity will be screened in the hydrothiolation of benchmark substrates (e.g., benzaldehyde, acetophenone, N-benzylideneaniline) with aromatic and aliphatic thiols under base-free conditions. Reaction progress will be monitored by NMR/GC, and key mechanistic steps—such as substrate binding via ligand dearomatization, S-H bond activation (potentially via transient thiolate coordination as indicated in recent group work), and C-S bond formation—will be interrogated through stoichiometric reactions, isolation of intermediates, and DFT calculations.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new catalytic application of the aromatization-dearomatization MLC paradigm, moving beyond (de)hydrogenation to direct C-S bond formation. It will provide fundamental insights into how cooperative ligand platforms can activate polar multiple bonds and mediate heteroatom transfer, expanding the synthetic toolbox for sustainable catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While MLC for (de)hydrogenation is well-established, its use for the catalytic hydrothiolation of carbonyls/imines is unprecedented. The proposed mechanism, leveraging ligand dearomatization for electrophile activation combined with transient thiolate coordination for nucleophile delivery, represents a novel, bio-inspired cooperative pathway distinct from classical Lewis acid or base catalysis.

proposals.title: Robust Pincer-Manganese Catalysts for the Hydrogenation of Nitriles to Primary Amines: Leveraging Metal-Ligand and Metal-Ligand-Substrate Cooperation for Industrial Application
proposals.motivation: The catalytic hydrogenation of nitriles to primary amines is a crucial industrial process for producing pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and polymers. Current industrial methods often rely on precious metal catalysts (e.g., Ru, Pt) or require stoichiometric, corrosive additives, generating significant waste. There is a pressing need for base-metal catalysts that are active under mild, additive-free conditions and are tolerant of diverse functional groups to enable greener chemical manufacturing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that manganese pincer complexes, designed with specific ligand frameworks that engage in both Metal-Ligand Cooperation (MLC) and transient Metal-Ligand-Substrate Cooperation (MLSC) with thiol additives, will provide a robust, scalable, and highly selective catalytic platform for the hydrogenation of structurally diverse nitriles to primary amines under industrially viable conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of Mn(I) pincer complexes bearing modified pyridine- or acridine-based PNP and PNN backbones, incorporating steric and electronic tuning. Catalytic performance will be evaluated in the hydrogenation of benchmark aliphatic and aromatic nitriles under base-free conditions, using H2 (1-50 bar) at 80-120°C. Building on Milstein's recent insights, we will systematically study the role of thiols (e.g., ethanethiol) as transient cooperative ligands, optimizing their concentration to accelerate the reaction while inhibiting deactivation pathways. Advanced kinetic studies, operando spectroscopy (IR, NMR), and DFT calculations will be employed to map the catalytic cycle and identify the key MLC/MLSC steps responsible for high activity and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new family of operationally simple, manganese-based catalysts for a fundamental chemical transformation, demonstrating their practical potential for nitrile hydrogenation. It will provide a deep mechanistic understanding of how cooperative ligand effects and transient substrate interactions can be harnessed to enhance the robustness and functional group tolerance of base-metal catalysts, moving them closer to industrial adoption.
proposals.novelty: While Mn-catalyzed hydrogenations are known, the deliberate optimization of pincer-Mn systems for nitrile hydrogenation by integrating permanent MLC (from the pincer backbone) with transient MLSC (from thiol additives) to achieve industrial-grade performance and substrate scope is unprecedented. This approach moves beyond proof-of-concept to address practical stability and scalability challenges.

proposals.title: Light-Driven Hydrogenation and Dehydrogenation via Metal-Ligand Cooperative Pincer Complexes Merged with Photoredox Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Milstein's work has established metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly with dearomatizable pincer ligands and transient thiol ligands, as a powerful paradigm for (de)hydrogenation catalysis. Separately, photoredox catalysis has emerged as a versatile tool for accessing reactive radical intermediates and driving redox-neutral transformations under mild conditions. Merging these fields could unlock new mechanistic pathways and catalytic cycles that are inaccessible thermally, expanding the scope of sustainable redox chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a photosensitizer with a dearomatizable/aromatizable pincer complex will enable light to drive the key ligand redox cycle, generating potent, yet selective, reductants or oxidants for challenging substrate activation under ambient conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and characterize known Mn(I) and Ru(II) pincer complexes featuring pyridine- or acridine-based dearomatizable ligands. These will be paired with established organic (e.g., phenothiazine, acridinium) or Ir(III)/Ru(II) polypyridyl photosensitizers in a dual catalytic system. Reactions will be performed under inert atmosphere with visible light irradiation (blue LEDs). We will systematically screen conditions for model reactions: the light-driven dehydrogenative coupling of alcohols to esters (using H2 acceptor) and the hydrogenation of challenging unsaturated substrates (e.g., polyaromatics, amides) using a sacrificial donor. Mechanistic studies will include Stern-Volmer quenching, EPR/spin-trapping for radicals, and detailed kinetic analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new platform for photochemical (de)hydrogenation that leverages ligand non-innocence, moving beyond traditional energy-transfer or single-electron-transfer pathways. It will provide fundamental insights into how light can be used to modulate MLC cycles, potentially leading to catalysts with improved selectivity and functional group tolerance under milder conditions than thermal analogues.
proposals.novelty: While photoredox catalysis is well-established, its direct integration with the aromatization-dearomatization cycle of pincer ligands for driving main catalytic steps is unexplored. The novelty lies in using light not just to generate a substrate radical, but to directly power the cooperative ligand's redox shuttle, creating a synergistic catalytic system.

proposals.title: Metal-Ligand Cooperation via Dearomatized Pincer Complexes for Catalytic C–H and N–H Bond Formation via Formal Dehydrogenative Coupling
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, Milstein's work on metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) using dearomatized pincer complexes has revolutionized bond activation (e.g., H2, N2O) and (de)hydrogenation catalysis. However, the dearomatized state, a potent nucleophile, has been used primarily for substrate activation. The inverse application—using this nucleophilicity directly for catalytic bond *formation*—remains largely unexplored and could unlock new, atom-economical synthetic pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the nucleophilic dearomatized arm of Ru(II) or Mn(I) pincer complexes can catalytically deprotonate acidic C–H or N–H bonds, generating a transient metal-bound nucleophile and a ligand-stabilized electrophile, which then recombine to form new bonds via a formal dehydrogenative coupling mechanism.
proposals.method: We will synthesize Ru(II) and Mn(I) complexes based on established PNP and PNN pincer frameworks (e.g., Milstein's iPr-PNN). Their reactivity with substrates containing activated C–H bonds (e.g., terminal alkynes, methylazaarenes) and N–H bonds (e.g., amines, amides) will be studied stoichiometrically via NMR and X-ray crystallography. Catalytic cycles will be developed for reactions such as the hydroamination of alkynes or the α-alkylation of nitriles with alcohols, leveraging the ligand's aromatization/dearomatization cycle. Key parameters include base-free conditions, hydrogen acceptor choice, and the role of transient cooperative ligands (e.g., thiols) to modulate reactivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for MLC, demonstrating catalytic bond-forming reactions driven directly by the ligand's dearomatized state. It will deliver novel, base-free catalytic methodologies for C–C and C–N bond formation. The findings will deepen the fundamental understanding of the interplay between ligand nucleophilicity and metal-centered processes in catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While dearomatized complexes are known for bond activation, their direct, catalytic deployment for constructive C–H/N–H functionalization is a conceptually inverse and unexplored application of the MLC principle, moving beyond classical (de)hydrogenation.

proposals.title: Metal-Free Acceptorless Dehydrogenation via Cooperative Thiol-Based Heterogeneous and Radical Pathways
proposals.motivation: Acceptorless dehydrogenation (AD) is a pivotal atom-economical route for synthesizing unsaturated compounds and releasing H2, but state-of-the-art systems rely heavily on homogeneous, often precious, metal complexes with specialized pincer ligands. This creates challenges in catalyst separation, cost, and potential metal contamination. Exploring fundamentally different, metal-free catalytic paradigms for AD could lead to more sustainable and easily scalable processes, aligning with green chemistry principles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the metal-ligand cooperative principles, particularly involving thiol/disulfide redox couples and radical intermediates as demonstrated in Milstein's recent work, can be translated to entirely metal-free systems using engineered heterogeneous matrices or enzymatic scaffolds to drive efficient AD reactions.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize solid supports (e.g., mesoporous silica, functionalized polymers) covalently grafted with cooperative thiol/disulfide and amine/imide pairs inspired by pincer ligand motifs. These materials will be tested in model AD reactions (e.g., of alcohols to carbonyls). Parallel approaches will involve screening engineered enzymes (e.g., disulfide oxidoreductases) and probing for radical chain mechanisms using initiators (e.g., AIBN) and radical traps, with detailed kinetic and spectroscopic analysis (EPR, operando IR) to elucidate mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for non-metal-based AD catalysis, potentially identifying new classes of heterogeneous, biocatalytic, or radical-chain catalysts. It will expand the conceptual framework of 'cooperativity' beyond metal-ligand partnerships to include organic and biomolecular systems, offering alternative strategies for sustainable dehydrogenation.
proposals.novelty: While metal-ligand cooperation is well-established, its deliberate abstraction to create fully metal-free, cooperative catalytic sites for AD is unexplored. The direct translation of mechanistic insights from homogeneous metal-thiol cooperation to heterogeneous and radical organic catalysis represents a significant conceptual leap.

proposals.title: Bio-Inspired Metal-Ligand Cooperation: Pincer Complexes with Transient, NAD(P)H-Mimetic Hydride-Accepting Ligands for Catalytic Dehydrogenation
proposals.motivation: Enzymatic dehydrogenations, powered by cofactors like NAD(P)+, achieve exceptional efficiency and selectivity under mild conditions through sophisticated hydride transfer mechanisms. In contrast, synthetic molecular catalysts for acceptorless dehydrogenation often rely on expensive noble metals and can suffer from deactivation or limited substrate scope. Bridging this gap by integrating enzymatic design principles into robust organometallic systems could unlock new, sustainable catalytic transformations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that designing pincer-ligated base-metal complexes (e.g., Mn, Fe, Ca) featuring ligands capable of reversible, cofactor-like hydride acceptance via Metal-Ligand Cooperation (MLC) will create efficient, bio-inspired catalysts for the dehydrogenation of challenging substrates like alcohols and amines under base-free conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel pincer ligands with integrated, redox-active heterocyclic units (e.g., pyridinium, quinolinium) that mimic the hydride-accepting core of NAD(P)+. These will be complexed to metals central to Milstein's recent work (Mn, Ca) and to earth-abundant Fe. Catalytic activity will be tested in the dehydrogenation of benzyl alcohol and N-heterocycle synthesis (e.g., from amino alcohols). Kinetic studies, including H/D exchange and inhibition experiments with thiol additives (inspired by recent findings on transient ligand effects), will be used to probe the proposed hydride transfer via ligand-centered reduction, distinguishing it from classical metal-centered pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for dehydrogenation catalysts based on biomimetic, ligand-centered hydride reservoirs. It will expand the scope of Metal-Ligand Cooperation beyond proton transfer to include formal hydride transfer, potentially leading to more selective and stable catalysts using abundant metals. The project will provide fundamental insights into mimicking enzymatic cofactor function within a synthetic coordination sphere.
proposals.novelty: While MLC for bond activation is well-established, the explicit design of pincer ligands to act as transient, NAD(P)+-like hydride acceptors in catalytic cycles is unexplored. This approach uniquely combines the robustness of organometallic pincer complexes with the elegant hydride-transfer logic of biocatalysis.

proposals.title: Catalytic C–C Bond Formation via In Situ Organometallic Nucleophile Generation Enabled by Metal–Ligand and Calcium–Ligand Cooperation
proposals.motivation: Numerous valuable organic transformations, such as nucleophilic additions to unsaturated nitriles or imines, rely on stoichiometric organometallic reagents (e.g., Grignard, organolithium). This generates significant stoichiometric waste, limits functional group tolerance, and reduces atom economy. Developing catalytic cycles where the organometallic nucleophile is generated in situ from abundant, simple precursors would represent a major advance in sustainable synthesis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bifunctional pincer complexes, leveraging metal–ligand cooperation (MLC) and calcium–ligand cooperation (CaLC), can catalytically activate C–H bonds in simple organic precursors (e.g., nitriles, arenes) to generate reactive organometallic nucleophiles in situ, enabling catalytic C–C bond formation without stoichiometric organometallics.
proposals.method: The project will focus on adapting Milstein's established MLC platforms (e.g., Ru, Mn, Fe pincer complexes) and CaLC systems. We will first design catalytic cycles where a metal hydride (M–H), generated from H2, undergoes C–H metalation of a substrate (e.g., acetonitrile) to form the key organometallic nucleophile. This species will then add to an electrophile (e.g., an unsaturated nitrile or imine), followed by product release and regeneration of M–H via H2 activation. Critical to success will be ligand design (potentially incorporating thiol/thiolate motifs as transient cooperative ligands) and the use of calcium complexes to modulate reactivity and facilitate substrate activation, as seen in N2O and H2 activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for catalytic C–C bond formation that circumvents pre-formed organometallics, directly advancing green chemistry principles. It will expand the scope of metal–ligand cooperation to include the catalytic generation of nucleophiles, a fundamental synthetic operation. The successful merger of MLC and CaLC concepts could open new avenues in main group–transition metal cooperative catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While MLC is well-established for (de)hydrogenation, its application to catalytically generate organometallic nucleophiles from C–H bonds in a productive catalytic cycle is unexplored. The synergistic integration of calcium–ligand cooperation, a recent Milstein group innovation, to facilitate this process is a highly novel and non-obvious strategy.

proposals.title: Ligand-Modulated Palladium Pincer Complexes for Enhanced Activity and Selectivity in Base-Free Hydrogenation Catalysis
proposals.motivation: Palladium-catalyzed hydrogenations are fundamental in organic synthesis, yet often require high catalyst loadings, strong bases, or specific ligands to achieve efficiency and selectivity. Recent work by Milstein and others on metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly with thiols and earth-abundant metals, has demonstrated that ligand design can dramatically accelerate reactions and enable base-free conditions. Applying these MLC principles to palladium catalysis, a well-established but often unoptimized system, presents a clear opportunity to develop more sustainable and effective catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by incorporating hemilabile or cooperative donor groups (e.g., thiols, amines) into phosphine-based pincer ligands for palladium, we can create catalysts that operate via unique MLC pathways, leading to significantly reduced catalyst loadings and improved chemo- or regioselectivity in the hydrogenation of polar unsaturated bonds under base-free conditions.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of palladium pincer complexes featuring P^N^P, P^S^P, and related frameworks, where the central donor is varied (e.g., NR2, SR, O). Their catalytic performance will be systematically evaluated in model hydrogenation reactions of imines, nitriles, and unsaturated nitriles, benchmarking against known Pd/phosphine systems. Reaction kinetics, poisoning studies, and detailed mechanistic investigations (including NMR spectroscopy, deuterium labeling, and DFT calculations) will be employed to correlate ligand structure with activity, delineate operative mechanisms (classical vs. MLC), and identify key intermediates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish structure-activity relationships for a new class of Pd pincer catalysts operating via ligand cooperation, directly translating concepts from Milstein's work on Mn and Ca to a noble metal context. It will provide a blueprint for minimizing Pd loading and eliminating stoichiometric bases in hydrogenation, contributing to more atom-economical and environmentally benign synthetic protocols.
proposals.novelty: While Pd/phosphine catalysts are ubiquitous, their systematic redesign with cooperative, hemilabile donors in a pincer architecture to intentionally enable base-free, MLC-type hydrogenation cycles is largely unexplored and represents a novel fusion of classical palladium catalysis with contemporary ligand-design principles.

proposals.title: Metal–Ligand Cooperative Iron Pincer Catalysis for the Chemoselective Hydrogenation of Esters in the Presence of Sensitive Functional Groups
proposals.motivation: The selective reduction of esters to alcohols in the presence of other reducible functionalities (e.g., alkenes, nitriles, carbonyls) remains a significant challenge in synthetic chemistry, often requiring multi-step protection/deprotection sequences or precious metal catalysts. Developing base-metal catalysts, particularly iron-based systems, for such chemoselective transformations is highly desirable for sustainable synthesis. Milstein's recent work on metal–ligand cooperation (MLC), including the use of thiols as transient ligands and the demonstration of calcium-ligand cooperation, provides a foundational platform to address this selectivity problem with earth-abundant metals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iron pincer complex operating via a metal–ligand cooperative mechanism can be tuned to selectively hydrogenate the ester carbonyl over other common reducible groups, leveraging the unique reactivity of the ligand scaffold and the transient coordination of modulators (like thiols) to control chemoselectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and screen a library of known and novel iron pincer complexes, primarily based on PNP and PNN frameworks, for the hydrogenation of benchmark ester substrates containing alkenes, alkynes, nitriles, and ketones. Reaction conditions (temperature, H2 pressure, solvent, additive) will be systematically varied, with a specific focus on testing the effect of thiol additives, inspired by recent inhibition/acceleration studies, as potential chemoselectivity modulators. Reaction progress and selectivity will be monitored by GC-MS and NMR spectroscopy, and catalyst speciation will be studied via in-situ IR and NMR to probe operative mechanisms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first systematic study of chemoselectivity in ester hydrogenation using iron MLC catalysts, potentially delivering a practical, selective tool for complex molecule synthesis. It will expand the understanding of how transient ligands and cooperative frameworks can be harnessed for selectivity control, bridging Milstein's work on MLC, thiol modulation, and base-metal hydrogenation catalysis.
proposals.novelty: While iron-catalyzed ester hydrogenation is known, pre-2026 work has not deeply explored chemoselectivity over other sensitive functionalities using MLC iron pincer systems. The deliberate application of thiols or other modulators, informed by recent mechanistic insights from the Milstein group, to *steer* selectivity in this context is a novel and non-obvious strategy.

proposals.title: Iron-Pincer Complexes and Esterases in Cooperative Tandem Catalysis: Merging Metal-Ligand Cooperation with Biocatalysis for Selective Ester Hydrogenation
proposals.motivation: The development of sustainable, earth-abundant metal catalysts for hydrogenation is a central goal in green chemistry. While Milstein's group has pioneered metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) in pincer complexes for H2 activation and hydrogenation, and enzymatic catalysis offers exquisite selectivity under mild conditions, the integration of these two powerful paradigms remains largely unexplored. Creating synergistic tandem or hybrid systems could unlock new, efficient pathways for transforming abundant feedstocks like esters, which are challenging to hydrogenate selectively with single catalysts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an iron-pincer complex, operating via MLC for H2 activation, can be effectively coupled with an esterase in a one-pot tandem system, where the enzyme selectively hydrolyzes an ester to an acid, which is then efficiently hydrogenated to the alcohol by the iron catalyst. The compatibility and potential inhibitory effects between the two catalytic cycles will be governed by judicious ligand design on the metal complex.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of iron(II) pincer complexes featuring modified, enzyme-compatible ligand backbones (e.g., incorporating polar, non-inhibitory groups) based on Milstein's established platforms. Their individual catalytic performance in the hydrogenation of carboxylic acids will be benchmarked. Concurrently, we will screen commercial and engineered esterases for activity and stability under the reaction conditions. The optimal iron complex and esterase will then be combined in a one-pot system for the direct hydrogenation of esters (e.g., ethyl levulinate to γ-valerolactone). Reaction parameters (solvent, pH buffer, temperature, order of addition) will be optimized to achieve synergy, and detailed mechanistic studies (kinetics, inhibition assays, deuterium labeling) will probe the interaction between the catalytic cycles.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational proof-of-concept for merging MLC-based earth-abundant metal catalysis with enzymatic catalysis, a novel hybrid approach. It will deliver a new, potentially greener methodology for the selective reduction of ester bonds and provide fundamental insights into the compatibility and cooperative effects between organometallic and biological catalysts in the same pot.
proposals.novelty: While both iron MLC catalysts and esterases are known independently, their integration into a cooperative tandem system is unprecedented. The novelty lies in designing the metal complex's ligand sphere to be non-toxic to the enzyme while maintaining MLC activity, creating a true hybrid catalytic cycle rather than a simple sequential process.

proposals.title: Single-Molecule Fluorescence Microscopy of Iron Pincer Catalysts: Probing Heterogeneity and Metal-Ligand Cooperation in Hydrogenation
proposals.motivation: Heterogeneous catalyst behavior, where individual molecules exhibit distinct activities and stabilities, is a major frontier in catalysis but remains largely unexplored for molecular transition metal complexes. Pre-2026, the Milstein group has pioneered metal-ligand cooperation (MLC), particularly with thiols and other functional groups, to enable unique (de)hydrogenation pathways. Understanding how MLC manifests and fluctuates at the single-molecule level could reveal the fundamental origins of catalyst efficiency and deactivation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-molecule studies of an iron pincer catalyst, featuring a thiol-based cooperative ligand, will reveal significant temporal heterogeneity in hydrogenation activity directly linked to dynamic, non-covalent ligand interactions and transient metal-ligand cooperative states.
proposals.method: We will synthesize an iron pincer complex, analogous to known MLC systems, functionalized with a fluorophore (e.g., Cy5) for single-molecule fluorescence microscopy. We will immobilize these catalysts on a functionalized glass surface and image them under catalytic conditions for alkene hydrogenation using total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy. By tracking the fluorescence intensity and lifetime of individual catalysts in real-time, we will correlate 'on' and 'off' catalytic cycles (via product-linked quenching or local environmental changes) with stochastic binding/unbinding events of the thiol cooperative ligand, using established single-molecule kinetic analysis algorithms.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, real-time visualization of metal-ligand cooperative dynamics in action for a homogeneous catalyst, quantifying the heterogeneity inherent in these systems. It will establish a novel experimental bridge between bulk catalytic measurements and the stochastic behavior of individual catalyst molecules, offering mechanistic insights beyond ensemble averages.
proposals.novelty: While single-molecule studies exist for enzymes and some heterogeneous catalysts, applying this technique to a well-defined molecular organometallic system operating via MLC is unprecedented. It moves beyond inferring mechanism from bulk kinetics to directly observing the transient species and non-productive states that define catalyst performance.

ideas.title: Catalytic oxidative cross-coupling of two distinct alcohols to asymmetric esters
ideas.description: This redesigned system enables the direct synthesis of unsymmetrical esters from two different alcohol feedstocks via an integrated oxidative coupling mechanism. A bifunctional catalyst sequentially activates and dehydrogenates one alcohol to generate a reactive acyl intermediate, which is then captured by the second, distinct alcohol nucleophile. The process employs a hydrogen acceptor or mild oxidant to drive the reaction thermodynamically, bypassing the need for pre-formed acid derivatives. Selectivity for the cross-product over homo-coupling dimers is controlled through catalyst design and careful matching of alcohol oxidation and nucleophilic addition rates.

ideas.title: Photoredox-Catalyzed Dehydrogenative Lactonization via Hydrogen Atom Transfer (HAT)
ideas.description: This research direction merges photoredox catalysis with intramolecular lactone formation to develop a mild, selective method for synthesizing lactones from diols or hydroxy acids. By employing a dual catalytic system—a photoactive catalyst paired with a hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) co-catalyst—light irradiation generates a radical intermediate that abstracts a hydrogen atom, triggering an intramolecular dehydrogenative cyclization. This approach operates under ambient temperature and visible light, avoiding traditional metal oxidants and high temperatures. The synergy allows for exceptional functional group tolerance and the potential to selectively target specific C-H bonds in polyol substrates, enabling the synthesis of complex, strained, or biologically relevant lactone architectures that are difficult to access via conventional thermal oxidation.

ideas.title: Shifting Reaction Equilibrium via a Photochemical Acceptor System
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a hydrogen acceptor must be a stoichiometric reagent that remains in the system. Drawing an analogy from plant physiology and photosynthesis, where light energy drives the continuous removal of electrons via a coupled acceptor system (e.g., ferredoxin/NADP+), we propose adapting this principle to synthetic hydrogenation. The adapted research idea is to design a photochemical cycle for hydrogen transfer. A photocatalyst absorbs light to create a high-energy state capable of abstracting hydrogen from a substrate. This hydrogen is transferred not to the final product directly, but to a temporary, regenerable molecular carrier. This carrier then undergoes a fast, irreversible step to deliver the hydrogen to the ultimate acceptor (the desired product), thereby regenerating itself. The key innovation is using light energy to power the continuous 'shuttling' of hydrogen away from the equilibrium-limited primary reaction, effectively shifting the equilibrium and allowing the reaction to proceed under milder conditions without a net stoichiometric acceptor. This creates an acceptor-free system in the traditional sense, replacing it with a light-driven, catalytic pumping mechanism.

ideas.title: Functional Group-Guided Selective Cross-Esterification of Complex Alcohols
ideas.description: This research direction develops catalytic systems for the selective cross-esterification of two distinct, functionally complex alcohols. The core innovation is to exploit the sensitive functional groups—traditionally avoided as incompatible—as chemical handles to influence reaction pathway selectivity, potentially through substrate pre-coordination, steric gating, or electronic modulation. This enables the synthesis of asymmetrical esters from complex alcohol building blocks with high selectivity that is dictated by the functional groups themselves, moving beyond simple catalyst tolerance to functional group-directed synthesis. The goal is to establish design rules for converting challenging polyfunctional substrates into precise coupling partners for modular ester assembly.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Selective Catalysts for Secondary Alcohol Dehydrogenation
ideas.description: This research direction integrates catalyst design for secondary alcohol dehydrogenation with miniaturized, parallel reaction screening in microtiter plates. The core innovation is the systematic construction and testing of catalyst libraries against a diverse array of secondary alcohol substrates—including sterically hindered and electronically varied examples—in a single high-throughput experiment. This approach will rapidly map catalyst performance across chemical space, revealing critical selectivity trends for challenging transformations like the selective dehydrogenation of unsymmetrical diols or alcohols bearing sensitive functional groups. The resulting dataset will directly inform the iterative design of next-generation, highly selective catalysts optimized for specific substrate classes.

ideas.title: Mechanistic Elucidation of Acid-Modulated Catalytic Cycles via Advanced Kinetic Analysis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the deliberate addition of controlled acidic additives with comprehensive kinetic and spectroscopic mechanistic studies. The core objective is not merely to see if acid enhances rates, but to employ detailed kinetic analysis (e.g., variable time normalization analysis, Eyring studies, isotopic labeling) and in-situ spectroscopy to construct a complete mechanistic map of how acid modulates each elementary step. This approach will reveal whether acid acts as a co-catalyst, a proton shuttle, or a modifier of the catalyst's resting state, providing fundamental insights that transcend the binary 'base-free vs. not' paradigm. The outcome will be a predictive framework for using acids as precise thermodynamic and kinetic levers in catalyst design.

ideas.title: pH-Modulated Hydrogen Acceptor Systems for Enhanced Reaction Control
ideas.description: This research direction investigates the strategic combination of pH-modifying additives with carefully selected hydrogen acceptors to overcome the limitations of neutral, acceptor-free conditions. By designing acceptors whose binding affinity, stability, or mode of action is sensitive to pH, the system gains a dynamic control lever. This allows for the acidic or basic additive to not only influence the rate or selectivity of the primary reaction but also to modulate the acceptor's efficiency in shifting the equilibrium or enabling lower-temperature operation. The synergy enables a temporally controlled reaction pathway, where conditions can be optimized sequentially for activation and then for completion, achieving performance unattainable with either strategy alone.

ideas.title: Continuous Flow Platform for Acid-Additive Tuning of Base-Free Esterification Catalysis
ideas.description: This research integrates the concept of probing base-free esterification with controlled acidic additives into a continuous flow chemistry system. The flow reactor allows for precise, rapid, and automated variation of acid additive concentration, residence time, and temperature in a single integrated experiment. This enables the high-throughput generation of kinetic and mechanistic data to construct a detailed catalytic phase map, revealing how small amounts of acid can temporarily activate or deactivate the catalyst without requiring a stoichiometric base. The continuous system uniquely allows for the study and potential application of self-correcting, oscillatory, or gradient-based catalytic pathways that leverage transient acidity for enhanced selectivity or rate acceleration in a nominally base-free process.

ideas.title: Operando Spectroscopy-Guided Design of Cooperative Proton-Responsive Catalysts
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the design of enzyme-inspired bifunctional catalysts with proton-responsive sites directly with operando spectroscopic monitoring. By using techniques like operando IR or NMR to track protonation states and substrate transformations simultaneously during catalysis, one can observe how the concerted action of the designed cooperative sites evolves in real time. This feedback loop allows for the refinement of catalyst structures based on mechanistic insights gained under operational conditions, rather than inferred from static models. The ultimate goal is to develop a general strategy for rationally engineering cooperative catalysts where spectroscopic data directly informs the optimization of proton relay networks for superior activity and selectivity.

ideas.title: Pilot-Scale Catalytic Synthesis of Macrocyclic Lactone Fragrances via Intramolecular Dehydrogenative Coupling
ideas.description: This research fuses catalyst development for intramolecular lactonization with industrial process scale-up to establish a novel, efficient pilot-scale synthesis for macrocyclic fragrance esters. The core innovation is the application of a dehydrogenative coupling catalyst—typically studied for small-scale lactone formation from diols/hydroxy acids—to the continuous-flow synthesis of prized macrocyclic musks (e.g., ambrettolide, globalide) from renewable long-chain substrates. This approach bypasses traditional stoichiometric reagents and high-dilution conditions, significantly improving atom economy and enabling scalable production. The work will optimize catalyst longevity and separation for continuous operation, directly translating a selective synthetic methodology into a viable industrial fragrance manufacturing process.

ideas.title: Systematic Brønsted Acid Analysis for Base-Free Catalytic Enhancement
ideas.description: This study moves beyond simply adding acid by implementing a rigorous Brønsted acid analysis within the base-free catalytic system. A homologous series of acids with a wide, known range of pKa values will be used as additives under otherwise identical conditions. The reaction performance will be plotted against acid pKa to generate a Brønsted correlation, which will reveal if the catalysis is specifically sensitive to proton-donating ability. This approach definitively tests whether acid enhancement is a general medium effect or a specific proton-transfer event, identifies the precise acidity required, and provides critical mechanistic insight into the role of protons in the supposedly base-free process.

ideas.title: Apply stopped-flow and freeze-quench techniques to capture transient catalytic intermediates
ideas.description: This research adapts rapid kinetic techniques from enzymology—specifically stopped-flow and freeze-quench methods—to elucidate the full catalytic cycle in homogeneous catalysis. In enzymology, these techniques are used with spectroscopic probes (EPR, Mössbauer, UV-Vis) to trap and characterize millisecond-to-second scale intermediates, reconstructing complete reaction coordinates. The adapted idea applies these methods to capture transient organometallic intermediates (e.g., metal-hydrides, substrate-bound complexes, high-valent states) during catalysis, using stopped-flow UV-Vis and freeze-quench EPR/Mössbauer spectroscopy. This approach will map the free energy landscape beyond the rate-determining step, providing a mechanistic picture analogous to that achieved for complex enzymatic systems.

ideas.title: Quantitative Descriptor-Driven Optimization of Hemilabile Ligands for Predictive Catalysis
ideas.description: This project establishes a closed-loop, data-driven pipeline to move hemilabile ligand design from qualitative intuition to quantitative prediction. A computational descriptor quantifying hemilability is developed by correlating DFT-calculated parameters with experimental spectroscopic and thermodynamic data from model complexes. This descriptor is then used to design a targeted library of phosphorus-based ligands, which are synthesized using parallel methods. Each ligand is screened in a model catalytic transformation under standardized conditions using high-throughput kinetic analysis, directly linking the descriptor value to catalytic rate, selectivity, and functional group tolerance. The resulting dataset enables the creation of predictive models for catalyst performance based on ligand structure.

ideas.title: Mechanistic deconvolution of catalyst deactivation by sensitive alcohol functionalities
ideas.description: This study systematically evaluates catalyst tolerance by not only screening conversion of functionalized alcohols but also employing in-situ IR/NMR spectroscopy and parallel poisoning experiments to identify the precise chemical mechanism of deactivation for each problematic group. Competitive kinetic experiments between a benchmark alcohol and a functionalized analogue will quantify the relative rates of productive turnover versus inhibitory binding. The output is a detailed mechanistic map linking functional group identity to specific deactivation pathways, enabling the future design of more robust catalysts or tailored protective strategies.

ideas.title: Operando Kinetic-Spectroscopic Analysis of Membrane-Integrated Catalytic Reactors
ideas.description: This research direction integrates hydrogen-selective membranes directly into a spectroscopic reaction cell to perform operando kinetic and mechanistic studies. By continuously removing hydrogen, the system operates under a perpetual thermodynamic driving force, allowing the study of the catalytic cycle away from equilibrium. This setup will capture spectroscopic signatures and kinetic isotope effects of transient intermediates that are otherwise suppressed or unobservable at equilibrium, providing a complete and accurate mechanistic map. The resulting data will directly inform the optimal design of both the catalyst and the membrane reactor architecture for intensified chemical processes.

ideas.title: Adapting Allosteric Control from Protein Kinases for Cooperative Catalysis
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from the allosteric control mechanisms prevalent in protein kinases, this idea adapts the solution of ligand-induced conformational switching to synthetic catalysis. The analogous problem of achieving precise, on-demand activation of a catalytic function is solved in kinases through distal regulatory sites that mechanically reposition the active site. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to design bifunctional catalysts where the proton-responsive site is not merely a cooperative participant but an allosteric regulator. Protonation or deprotonation at this site would be engineered to induce a specific conformational change in the catalyst scaffold, which in turn optimizes the geometry and electronic state of the primary catalytic site for the following concerted reaction step, thereby introducing dynamic, stimulus-responsive control over catalytic activity and selectivity.

ideas.title: Enzyme-Mimetic Templating for Selective Cross-Esterification of Alcohols
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that coupling two identical alcohol molecules inevitably leads to a symmetrical product. By drawing an analogy from molecular biology—specifically the templated selectivity of DNA polymerase during replication—we propose a solution. In DNA replication, a single-stranded template directs the selective incorporation of complementary nucleotides from a pool of similar building blocks. Adapted to this chemical context, the idea is to design a synthetic molecular template (e.g., a carefully engineered supramolecular host or a metal-organic cage) that can simultaneously and selectively bind two different alcohol molecules via non-covalent interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonding, coordination, or hydrophobic effects). This template would act as an enzyme-mimetic active site, pre-organizing the two alcohols and a coupling agent in the correct geometry to favor the formation of the asymmetric cross-ester, while disfavoring the dimerization of either identical alcohol. The goal is to achieve selective cross-esterification in a system where the two alcohol substrates are chemically similar and would otherwise couple randomly.

ideas.title: Acidic Co-catalyst Modulation Inspired by Enzyme Cofactors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the base-free catalysis assumption by proposing the controlled addition of acid, drawing an analogy from enzymology where cofactors modulate enzyme activity. In the analogous field, enzymes often require cofactors like metal ions to optimize function by altering the local electrostatic environment. The adapted research idea treats acidic additives not as mere proton sources but as modular 'chemical cofactors'. By screening a library of weakly coordinating acidic species, the goal is to discover if they can pre-organize substrates, stabilize key transition states through non-covalent interactions, or create a beneficial local proton microenvironment, thereby enhancing catalytic performance in rate or selectivity without reverting to traditional base-assisted mechanisms.

ideas.title: Multi-modal operando spectroscopy with isotopic labeling to deconvolute metal-ligand redox steps in MLC catalysis
ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the ambiguity of single-technique operando studies by employing a tightly coupled, multi-modal spectroscopic setup. It simultaneously uses operando UV-Vis/NIR and XAS to directly correlate ligand-based electronic changes (dearomatization/aromatization) with metal oxidation state in real time. This core experiment is reinforced by parallel operando NMR studies on reactions with 13C-labeled substrates, providing a direct atomic-level trace of the key carbon center involved in the transformation. The combined data streams provide a robust, multi-dimensional fingerprint that definitively assigns spectroscopic features to the metal-ligand cooperative event, distinguishing it from background processes.

ideas.title: Adaptive Bifunctional Catalysts with Dynamic Microenvironment Control
ideas.description: This research designs next-generation cooperative catalysts where the critical proton-responsive site is not static but embedded within a dynamically tunable microenvironment. The catalyst scaffold uses conformationally flexible linkers that allow the relative positioning of functional groups to adapt upon substrate binding, mimicking induced fit. Furthermore, the local chemical environment surrounding the proton-responsive site (e.g., polarity, electrostatic potential) is designed to be adjustable via external stimuli or through interaction with the substrate itself, enabling precise, on-demand modulation of its acid-base properties. This approach aims to overcome the rigidity and contextual insensitivity of traditional bifunctional catalysts, delivering enzyme-like adaptability for a broader range of concerted reactions under varying conditions.

ideas.title: Adapting Protein Engineering's Directed Evolution to Electron-Donating Ligand Design
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy from protein engineering's directed evolution, a field that solves the problem of optimizing complex, multi-parameter biological functions by rapidly screening vast combinatorial libraries. The analogous solution involves iterative cycles of library generation and high-throughput functional screening to discover optimal architectures. Adapted to the original research seed, this approach proposes synthesizing combinatorial libraries of Ru complexes featuring modular, variable electron-donating ligand components (e.g., substituted N-heterocyclic carbenes, phosphines, or amine donors). These libraries would then be subjected to high-throughput screening using techniques like parallel cyclic voltammetry or microplate-based catalytic assays. The goal is to empirically and efficiently discover novel, high-performing electron-rich ligand architectures that maximize metal electron density and catalytic performance, moving beyond purely rational design to include an element of discovery through parallel experimentation.

ideas.title: Elucidate full catalytic mechanism via integrated time-resolved, operando, and trapping studies
ideas.description: This project employs an integrated strategy to map the complete catalytic cycle, including elusive intermediates. Ultrafast time-resolved X-ray absorption and infrared spectroscopy will capture the kinetics and electronic structure of transient species on picosecond to microsecond timescales. These experiments are coupled with continuous in operando monitoring via NMR and UV-vis under realistic catalytic conditions to validate the relevance of observed intermediates. Concurrently, computational modeling predicts viable trap points, guiding the design of chemical quench-trapping experiments to isolate and fully characterize key metastable states by crystallography and mass spectrometry.

ideas.title: Decoupling pH Sensitivity from Component Stability to Map Reaction Landscape
ideas.description: This project challenges the assumption that a reaction requires neutral conditions by systematically testing whether the constraint is inherent to the core transformation or an artifact of catalyst or reagent instability. A high-throughput screening platform will be used to vary proton activity (pH/pD/pKaH) across a wide range within a buffered, mixed-solvent system, while independently screening libraries of structurally diverse catalysts and reagents for stability. This approach decouples the variables, allowing for the identification of conditions where non-neutral additives enhance rate or selectivity without causing decomposition. The outcome will be a detailed mechanistic map of the reaction's pH dependence and a set of validated, robust non-neutral protocols if they are discovered to be beneficial.

ideas.title: Integrated Hydrogen Valorization via Anaerobic Digestion-Inspired Metabolic Engineering
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that hydrogen co-produced in industrial processes like chlor-alkali manufacturing is merely a waste product. By drawing an analogy from microbial ecology and wastewater treatment—specifically the syntrophic relationships in anaerobic digesters where hydrogen is a key intermediate consumed by partner organisms—we propose a novel integrated solution. The analogous solution involves managing microbial communities to consume waste hydrogen for beneficial product formation. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to design and implement a two-stage bioreactor or bio-electrochemical system directly coupled to the hydrogen-emitting industrial process. The second stage would use metabolically engineered microbes or microbial electrosynthesis cells to utilize the H2 as an energy source for reducing captured CO2, transforming two waste streams (H2 and CO2) into valuable platform chemicals or fuels, thereby increasing overall process efficiency and sustainability.

ideas.title: Enhancing Reaction Performance via Local pH Microenvironments
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a reaction requires globally neutral conditions by drawing an analogy from biochemistry, where enzymes create localized pH microenvironments to optimize catalysis. The analogous solution involves managing pH at the site of reaction rather than in the bulk medium. Adapted to this chemical research context, the idea is to explore if acidic or basic additives could enhance rate or selectivity by designing catalytic systems that generate controlled pH microenvironments at the molecular or nanoscale, thus achieving the beneficial effects of acidity/basicity without subjecting the entire reaction mixture to non-neutral conditions.

ideas.title: Catalytic membrane reactor with active H2 driving force management
ideas.description: This approach designs a catalytic membrane reactor where the hydrogen-producing reaction occurs in direct contact with a high-temperature, hydrogen-selective membrane. An integrated vacuum pump or a counter-current sweep gas stream actively maintains a low hydrogen partial pressure on the permeate side, maximizing the driving force for hydrogen extraction. This continuous and efficient removal of hydrogen directly from the reaction zone pushes the chemical equilibrium decisively towards desired products, such as in dehydrogenation or steam reforming reactions. The system is engineered to balance reaction kinetics, membrane permeability, and energy efficiency for a robust performance enhancement.

ideas.title: Apply Operando Fluorescence Microscopy to Track MLC Aromatization via a Bio-inspired Reporter System
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core solution from cell biology—using environment-sensitive fluorescent reporters to track specific biochemical events in real-time within a complex system—to the problem of monitoring metal-ligand cooperation (MLC) in catalysis. The analogous solution involves probes whose fluorescence changes upon a target molecular transformation, enabling spatiotemporal visualization. The adapted idea is to design a fluorescent reporter system sensitive to the aromaticity state of a key ligand in MLC. By integrating such a probe into the catalytic system or using it as an external sensor, operando fluorescence microscopy or spectroscopy can be employed to visualize the aromatization/dearomatization steps in real-time, providing unprecedented spatial and kinetic details of the catalytic cycle under working conditions.

ideas.title: Evaluate catalyst tolerance using a functional group compatibility matrix
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from medicinal chemistry—the use of systematic screening matrices to identify problematic chemical motifs—to the problem of evaluating catalyst tolerance. In drug discovery, PAINS profiles help filter non-specific compounds; here, a functional group compatibility matrix will be used to screen a catalyst against a library of alcohols containing diverse sensitive groups (alkenes, alkynes, epoxides, ketones, amines, halides, etc.). The resulting profile will provide a clear, rapid assessment of the catalyst's functional group tolerance, guiding substrate scope definition and revealing deactivation pathways, much like a PAINS profile guides lead compound selection.

ideas.title: Employ computational screening for ligand design using a materials science-inspired high-throughput virtual screening pipeline
ideas.description: This idea adapts the high-throughput virtual screening (HTVS) pipeline, a solution from materials science for discovering functional materials like battery components, to the problem of ligand design in chemistry. In the analogous field, large databases of inorganic crystal structures are computationally screened using DFT to predict properties critical for application before any lab work. The adapted solution involves creating a virtual library of ligand building blocks and employing automated, high-throughput DFT calculations to predict optimal ligand structures based on target properties such as geometric fit, electronic donation/acceptance, and thermodynamic stability. This computational pre-screening dramatically narrows the experimental search space, accelerating the discovery of high-performance ligands for catalysis, sensing, or therapeutics.

ideas.title: Membrane-Integrated Reactive Distillation for Enhanced Product Yield
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core principle of reactive distillation—integrating reaction and separation in one unit to shift equilibrium—to membrane technology. In reactive distillation, product removal via vaporization drives reactions forward. The analogous solution here is to integrate a catalytic reactor directly with hydrogen-selective membranes (e.g., palladium-based or microporous silica) to continuously remove H₂ as it is produced. This membrane-integrated reactive separation would intensify processes like alkane dehydrogenation or methanol reforming by perpetually lowering the hydrogen partial pressure in the reaction zone, thereby pushing the equilibrium towards desired products, improving conversion, and potentially reducing downstream separation costs.

ideas.title: Adapting Photolithography's Multi-Stage Inline Processing for Multi-Step Catalytic Ester Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea expands continuous flow ester production by drawing an analogy from semiconductor photolithography's integrated, multi-stage inline processing. The analogous solution involves a series of specialized, interconnected modules performing sequential operations on a moving substrate. Adapted to chemical synthesis, this translates to a modular flow reactor system designed for complex, multi-step catalytic ester production. The reaction stream would flow through a sequence of dedicated modules—each optimized for a specific stage like substrate activation, core catalytic transformation, and inline workup—with precise control over parameters in each unit. This enables continuous, end-to-end synthesis of sophisticated esters that require incompatible conditions or multiple catalysts in sequence, significantly improving efficiency, yield, and reproducibility over batch or single-stage flow methods.

ideas.title: Techno-economic screening and modular valorization of sub-economic hydrogen streams
ideas.description: This research develops a two-stage system to robustly identify and utilize truly wasted hydrogen. First, a screening framework analyzes industrial processes to distinguish between already-valorized co-products and genuine waste streams based on concentration, purity, flow stability, and local infrastructure. Second, for streams deemed sub-economic, it designs and integrates modular technologies—such as selective membranes and compact fuel cells—that can handle impurities and variable flow to convert this hydrogen into electricity, heat, or chemical precursors on-site, ensuring economic viability by matching the scale and quality of the source.

ideas.title: A multi-fidelity computational pipeline with targeted high-level theory and empirical correction for robust ligand design
ideas.description: This redesigned approach employs a tiered computational strategy to overcome the accuracy limitations of standard DFT. An initial high-throughput DFT screen identifies promising ligand candidates, which are then subjected to targeted, more accurate (but costly) ab initio calculations for precise energy ranking. Finally, a machine-learning model, trained on a curated dataset of high-quality experimental results and high-level calculations, applies system-specific corrections to the computational predictions, ensuring the final design recommendations are both computationally informed and empirically grounded.

ideas.title: High-Throughput Catalyst Screening via Droplet Microfluidics and In-Line Optical Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ultra-high-throughput screening paradigm from biomedical diagnostics and drug discovery to catalyst development. The analogous solution uses droplet microfluidics to create millions of isolated, picoliter-scale reaction vessels for screening compound libraries, coupled with in-line optical sorting. Adapted to catalysis, the research idea involves designing a microfluidic chip that generates droplets containing individual catalyst library members, substrates, and an optical reporter. After on-chip incubation, a high-speed optical detection system (e.g., based on absorbance or fluorescence of a reaction product) analyzes each droplet's contents. This allows for the rapid, parallel screening of vast catalyst libraries with extreme miniaturization, drastically reducing material costs and accelerating the discovery of novel catalytic materials.

ideas.title: Continuous-Flow Biocatalytic Synthesis of Fragrance Esters via Immobilized Enzyme Microreactor Arrays
ideas.description: This research adapts the continuous manufacturing and 'numbering up' solutions from pharmaceutical API production to the pilot-scale synthesis of valuable flavor and fragrance esters. The analogous pharmaceutical field solved scale-up challenges for sensitive syntheses by moving from batch to continuous-flow microreactor arrays with immobilized catalysts, enabling precise control and modular scale-out. The adapted idea is to develop a pilot-scale process centered on continuous-flow biocatalytic microreactors or packed-bed columns containing immobilized enzymes (e.g., lipases). This system would operate by pumping substrate alcohols and acids through an array of identical, optimized reactor modules. This approach transfers the benefits seen in pharmaceuticals—improved yield and purity via controlled residence time and temperature, efficient catalyst reuse, and flexible, scalable production—to fragrance ester synthesis, moving beyond traditional batch fermentation or chemical catalysis.

ideas.title: Enzyme-Inspired Immobilized Catalyst Cartridges for Modular Microreactor Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Point-of-Care Diagnostics to solve the scaling challenge for on-demand synthesis. The analogous field is Biomedical Engineering, where lateral flow assays use immobilized reagents on a strip within a disposable cartridge for simple, pump-free fluid handling and specific reactions. The analogous solution involves a sample wicking through sequential zones of immobilized antibodies to produce a visible result. This is adapted by designing modular, single-use microfluidic cartridges pre-loaded with an immobilized solid-phase catalyst in a defined reaction zone. Precursor solutions are injected and flow through the catalyst bed via capillary action or a simple syringe, generating the ester on-demand. The spent cartridge is discarded, eliminating catalyst recovery and reactor cleaning, which is ideal for infrequent, point-of-use synthesis applications where simplicity and minimal user expertise are paramount.

ideas.title: Modular catalyst cartridges for robust, maintainable point-of-use ester synthesis in microreactors
ideas.description: This redesigned system decouples the catalyst from the permanent microfluidic device by housing it within a separate, plug-and-play cartridge. The microreactor unit features a standardized fluidic interface to accept these disposable catalyst cartridges, which contain the structured catalyst on a monolithic or porous support. This design enables continuous operation by allowing users to replace only the deactivated catalyst component, significantly reducing waste and cost. Integrated pressure or optical sensors before and after the cartridge can monitor catalyst health, prompting replacement only when needed.

ideas.title: Self-Optimizing Flow Reactor with Redundancy for Robust Continuous Esterification
ideas.description: This system implements continuous catalytic ester production in a flow reactor equipped with in-line spectroscopic analytics for real-time monitoring of conversion and catalyst health. A control algorithm uses this data to dynamically adjust residence time, temperature, and flow rates to maintain optimal performance. If catalyst deactivation is detected, the system automatically initiates an in-situ regeneration cycle or switches flow to an identical, pre-regenerated parallel reactor module to ensure uninterrupted operation. This design directly addresses the critical failure mode of catalyst decay, creating a resilient, self-correcting production system.

ideas.title: Parallel microreactor array with integrated sensing for predictive catalyst screening
ideas.description: This approach uses a platform of dozens of parallel, sealed microreactors (100-500 µL volume) each equipped with magnetic stirring, precise resistive heating, and pressure sensors. Inline fiber-optic probes allow for real-time reaction monitoring via techniques like FTIR or Raman spectroscopy. By maintaining proper mixing and gas handling while operating in parallel, the system collects high-fidelity kinetic and stability data on catalyst libraries. This data directly informs scale-up decisions, overcoming the traditional gap between high-throughput discovery and practical catalyst performance.
